entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement: base class NOT NULL fails despite subclass field being set
entries.body:
JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement: base class NOT NULL fails despite subclass field being set

### Symptom

`org.hibernate.PropertyValueException: not-null property references a null or transient value for entity CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId`

The test code explicitly sets `cle.tenancyId = "test-tenant"`. Java field access confirms the value is set. The error is impossible — except it isn't.

### Context

JPA JOINED inheritance with a base class (`LedgerEntry`) and subclass (`CaseLedgerEntry`). Both declare a `tenancyId` field with `@Column(name = "tenancy_id", nullable = false)`. In JOINED inheritance, these map to separate columns on separate tables (`ledger_entry.tenancy_id` and `case_ledger_entry.tenancy_id`).

### What was tried (didn't work)

1. Setting `tenancyId` on the entity before calling `save()` — still fails
2. Using `em.persist()` directly instead of the repository's `save()` — still fails
3. Setting the base field via reflection workaround — works but is wrong
4. Clean rebuild (`mvn clean`) to rule out stale bytecode — still fails

### Root cause

Java field shadowing creates two separate fields: `LedgerEntry.tenancyId` and `CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId`. They share the same name but are different fields in the JVM.

Hibernate bytecode enhancement (active by default in Quarkus 3.x) intercepts field access on entity classes. When code does `cle.tenancyId = "value"`, the enhancement transparently routes to the subclass field. When code does `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId`, the enhancement ALSO routes to the subclass field — making the shadowing invisible to Java code.

But `em.persist()` reads field values via reflection on the actual declared fields. `LedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId")` reads the base class field — which was never set (all writes were routed to the subclass by enhancement). The `nullable = false` constraint on the base table column fires.

### Diagnostic

```java
CaseLedgerEntry cle = new CaseLedgerEntry();
cle.tenancyId = "from-subclass";

// Java access (enhanced — both route to subclass):
cle.tenancyId                           → "from-subclass"
((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId           → "from-subclass"

// Reflection (raw — reveals the actual field state):
CaseLedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId").get(cle) → "from-subclass"
LedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId").get(cle)     → null  // THE BUG
```

### Fix

Remove the shadowing field from the subclass. JPA JOINED inheritance maps the base class field to the base table automatically — the subclass does not need its own declaration.

```java
// BEFORE (broken):
public class CaseLedgerEntry extends LedgerEntry {
    @Column(name = "tenancy_id", nullable = false)
    public String tenancyId;  // SHADOWS LedgerEntry.tenancyId
}

// AFTER (correct):
public class CaseLedgerEntry extends LedgerEntry {
    // tenancyId inherited from LedgerEntry — no shadowing
}
```

Migration: drop the `tenancy_id` column from the join table (it's redundant with `ledger_entry.tenancy_id`).

### Why this is non-obvious

Hibernate's bytecode enhancement makes the shadowing completely invisible to Java code. Both `cle.tenancyId` and `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId` return the same value. The error message names the subclass entity but the actual null is on the base class — and you can't see it without reflection. The symptom appears impossible: "the field is null but I literally just set it."

### Why this fix

Removing the shadowing field is the only correct fix. Workarounds (setting the base field via reflection, using a special save method) are fragile — they couple application code to JPA internals and break when the entity hierarchy changes. The field belongs on the base class; the subclass inherits it.

### Alternatives considered

- **Reflection workaround in test helpers** — sets the base field directly. Works but couples tests to the field shadowing structure and masks the underlying design bug.
- **Setting both fields explicitly** — impossible via normal Java code because enhancement intercepts the access. Would require reflection on every write path.
- **Hibernate `@Access(AccessType.PROPERTY)` with getter/setter** — might route correctly but changes the access strategy for all fields and is a much larger change.

### Caveats

- This only triggers when bytecode enhancement is active. Without enhancement (e.g. plain Hibernate outside Quarkus), both `cle.tenancyId` and `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId` access their respective declared fields — the shadowing is visible and the developer would naturally set both.
- The fix requires a Flyway migration to drop the redundant column from the join table.

**See also:** [GE-20260609-58e5d9](GE-20260609-58e5d9.md) — JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping

entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping — setting child field leaves parent nullable=false column null
entries.body:
JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping — setting child field leaves parent nullable=false column null

## Context

A JPA JOINED inheritance hierarchy where the child entity redeclares a field already declared in
the parent entity — both with `@Column(nullable = false)`. The child table gets a denormalized copy
of the column (e.g. for a local index to avoid joins). This pattern is intentional for performance
but creates a hidden trap for external writers.

Example from casehub platform:
- `LedgerEntry.tenancyId @Column(name="tenancy_id", nullable=false)` — in `ledger_entry` table
- `CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId @Column(name="tenancy_id", nullable=false, length=64)` — in `case_ledger_entry` table

## Symptom

```
org.hibernate.PropertyValueException: not-null property references a null or transient value
  for entity io.casehub.ledger.model.CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId
```

Thrown even though the code explicitly sets `entry.tenancyId = "tenant-1"` before calling
`em.persist()`. The value is visibly non-null; the error says it is null.

## Root cause

Java field access is statically resolved by the reference type at compile time. When `entry`
is declared as `CaseLedgerEntry`:

```java
entry.tenancyId = value;   // resolves to CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId (shadow field)
```

This writes to the child's own field (mapped to `case_ledger_entry.tenancy_id`). The parent's
field `LedgerEntry.tenancyId` (mapped to `ledger_entry.tenancy_id`, also `nullable=false`) is
never written. Hibernate's nullability check validates BOTH the child's property AND the parent's
property — the parent's property is null → `PropertyValueException`.

## Fix

Set both fields explicitly from any external code that touches the entity:

```java
entry.tenancyId = tenancyId;                    // CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId (case_ledger_entry)
((LedgerEntry) entry).tenancyId = tenancyId;    // LedgerEntry.tenancyId (ledger_entry)
```

The explicit cast forces the Java compiler to emit `PUTFIELD LedgerEntry.tenancyId` — a
separate bytecode instruction targeting the parent class's field declaration, distinct from
the child's shadow field.

The correct long-term fix: remove the field redeclaration from the child entity. Use a
`@SecondaryTable` or a Hibernate `@Formula` to maintain the index without the shadow field.

## Why this is non-obvious

The call site `entry.tenancyId = value` looks complete. The entity compiles and CDI-deploys
without warnings. The failure surfaces at `em.persist()` with a message naming the child entity
and property — which ARE set — not the parent entity and property — which are NOT set.

The Hibernate nullability check message `entity CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId` refers to the
property-path in the entity class hierarchy, not the Java field that is actually null. This
leads to the investigation going in the wrong direction (inspecting the child's field assignment
rather than the parent's).

Any code that uses direct field access from outside the entity (common in JPA entities without
getters/setters) is silently broken when this pattern is in play. Only tests that actually
perform a database persist — not those that simply assign and read back — will catch it.

**See also:** [GE-20260612-1f4ed8](GE-20260612-1f4ed8.md) — JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement causes NOT NULL failure

entries.title: ReactiveMessageLedgerEntryRepository.findLatestByCorrelationId queries LedgerEntry.subjectId, not MessageLedgerEntry.channelId — seeds with only channelId set return silently empty
casehub-ledger LedgerProcessor build step blocks em.persist() on LedgerEntry subclasses — use LedgerEntryRepository.save()
entries.body:
casehub-ledger LedgerProcessor build step blocks em.persist() on LedgerEntry subclasses — use LedgerEntryRepository.save()

### Symptom
`IllegalStateException: LedgerEntry must be persisted through LedgerEntryRepository, which handles sequence allocation, enrichment, hashing, and signing. Direct em.persist() bypasses the entire save pipeline.`

Thrown at runtime when code calls `em.persist(entry)` on any `LedgerEntry` subclass (`CaseLedgerEntry`, `MessageLedgerEntry`, `WorkerDecisionEntry`, etc.).

### Context
A Quarkus extension using casehub-ledger, after upgrading to a SNAPSHOT that added a `LedgerProcessor` Quarkus build step. Any existing code that directly persisted `LedgerEntry` subclasses via `EntityManager.persist()` breaks with the above error.

### Root cause
`casehub-ledger` ships a `LedgerProcessor` Quarkus deployment build step (`validateDomainContentBytes`) that also adds a JPA `@PrePersist` interceptor to every `LedgerEntry` subclass. The interceptor throws `IllegalStateException` if it detects a direct persist (i.e. the entity was not saved through the `LedgerEntryRepository` save pipeline).

The intent: `LedgerEntryRepository.save()` handles sequence number allocation (via `ledger_subject_sequence` table), leaf hash computation, Merkle chain update, and agent signature — bypassing it produces invalid ledger entries.

### Fix
Replace `em.persist(entry)` + `em.flush()` with `ledgerRepo.save(entry, tenancyId)`:

```java
// ❌ Blocked since casehub-ledger SNAPSHOT (will throw at runtime):
em.persist(entry);
em.flush();

// ✅ Correct:
@Inject LedgerEntryRepository ledgerRepo;
// ...
ledgerRepo.save(entry, tenancyId);
// save() handles sequence allocation, Merkle hash, and signing; no manual flush needed.
```

Also remove `@LedgerPersistenceUnit EntityManager em` injection if it was only used for `persist()` + `flush()` and the `nextSequenceNumber()` query (the repo handles sequencing internally).

### Why this is non-obvious
The `IllegalStateException` message is clear once seen, but:
1. The validation is added at Quarkus augmentation time via a deployment build step — it does not appear in the `LedgerEntryRepository` interface Javadoc or any user-facing docs.
2. Code that was previously working (`em.persist()`) breaks silently if the SNAPSHOT is updated but the calling code is not.
3. The `@PrePersist` interceptor is injected by the Quarkus extension's deployment artifact (`casehub-ledger-deployment`) — the mechanism is invisible when reading the entity class.

### Why this fix
`LedgerEntryRepository.save()` is the only supported path because it atomically allocates the per-subject sequence number from `ledger_subject_sequence`, computes the Merkle leaf hash, and updates the Merkle frontier — operations that must be coordinated to maintain chain integrity. Any bypass produces a ledger entry with an incorrect or missing sequence number and hash.

### Caveats
- `LedgerEntryRepository.save()` triggers a full Merkle chain update — see GE-20260531-d2ed26 for concurrent-write implications.
- The `ledger_subject_sequence` table must exist in the database. In test contexts using `hibernate.database.generation=drop-and-create`, this native SQL table is not created from JPA entities — use `quarkus.hibernate-orm.<pu-name>.sql-load-script` to create it (see also GE-20260612-*).

**See also:** [GE-20260612-de141c](GE-20260612-de141c.md) — casehub-ledger LedgerProcessor build step requires domainContentBytes() on all LedgerEntry subclasses with @Column fields

entries.title: Static entity factory creates JPA child entities without multi-tenancy fields — NOT NULL INSERT fails
entries.body:
Static entity factory creates JPA child entities without multi-tenancy fields — NOT NULL INSERT fails

### Symptom

```
org.hibernate.exception.ConstraintViolationException: column "tenancy_id" violates not-null constraint
```

Entities persisted via a static factory or utility method fail on INSERT even though the owning
parent entity has `tenancyId` set correctly.

### Context

`LedgerMerkleTree.append(digest, currentFrontier, subjectId)` is a pure static utility that
builds new `LedgerMerkleFrontier` nodes. It has no knowledge of tenancy context. The caller
in `ReactiveLedgerEntryJpaRepository.save()` sets `tenancyId` on the `LedgerEntry` being saved
but forgot to propagate it to the frontier nodes returned by `append()`. The nodes go into
`session.persist()` with `tenancyId = null`, violating the NOT NULL constraint.

### Root cause

Static factory and utility methods that create JPA entities operate outside any tenancy context.
Multi-tenancy fields (`tenancyId`, `tenantKey`, etc.) are caller responsibilities, not factory
responsibilities. The omission is invisible until the `session.persist()` flush fires — and only
on code paths where the NOT NULL column is actually written.

### Fix

Propagate multi-tenancy fields from the parent entity onto all child entities immediately after
the factory call, before any `session.persist()`:

```java
final List<LedgerMerkleFrontier> newFrontier =
    LedgerMerkleTree.append(e.digest, currentFrontier, e.subjectId);
newFrontier.forEach(node -> node.tenancyId = e.tenancyId);  // ← propagate before persist
// ... then session.persist(node) for each
```

### Why this is non-obvious

The parent entity (`LedgerEntry`) has `tenancyId` set correctly and was used for all prior
operations. The factory method looks complete — it takes all the structural parameters it needs.
The tenancy propagation step is easy to forget because it is not part of the factory's contract
and there is no type system or annotation to enforce it.

entries.title: Hibernate named query gains a parameter in one repo — sibling repo call site silently unbound until runtime
entries.body:
Hibernate named query gains a parameter in one repo — sibling repo call site silently unbound until runtime

### Symptom

```
org.hibernate.QueryParameterException: No argument for named parameter ':tenancyId'
  at ReactiveSqmQueryImpl.executeReactiveUpdate
```

All message dispatches fail with HTTP 500 when `casehub.ledger.hash-chain.enabled=true`.

### Context

`LedgerMerkleFrontier.deleteBySubjectAndLevel` is a `@NamedQuery` defined in `casehub-ledger`.
The ledger repo added a `:tenancyId` predicate to the query. `ReactiveLedgerEntryJpaRepository.save()`
in `casehub-qhorus` (a sibling repo) calls this named query but was never updated to bind the new parameter.
The call site compiled and deployed without error — the unbound parameter only surfaces at runtime
when the query executes.

### Root cause

JPA named queries are resolved by name at runtime, not by signature at compile time. When the
query definition in one repo gains a new parameter, every call site in every sibling repo that
uses the same named query must be manually updated. There is no compile-time or build-time
enforcement — neither the Java compiler nor the Maven build reports the mismatch.

### Fix

Bind the new parameter at every call site that uses the named query:

```java
session.createNamedQuery("LedgerMerkleFrontier.deleteBySubjectAndLevel")
    .setParameter("subjectId", e.subjectId)
    .setParameter("level", node.level)
    .setParameter("tenancyId", e.tenancyId)  // ← add when query gains this param
    .executeUpdate()
```

### Why this is non-obvious

The build succeeds. The CDI wiring is correct. The error only appears on a specific code
path (`hash-chain.enabled=true`) that may not be covered by standard CI tests. The exception
message names the parameter (`':tenancyId'`) but gives no indication of which call site is
missing the binding — a classpath-wide search is required to find the stale call site.

**See also:** [GE-20260617-54b75b](GE-20260617-54b75b.md) — Hibernate Reactive Panache repo.update() converts positional ?N params to named params — unbound at runtime

entries.title: Ledger count assertions in @QuarkusTest must filter by LedgerEntry subclass
entries.body:
Ledger count assertions in @QuarkusTest must filter by LedgerEntry subclass

Ledger entry count assertions that rely on `findBySubjectId().size()` without filtering by subclass will fail intermittently. Multiple services can write entries to the same subject chain (e.g. safety officer notification, escalation, resolution observers), and the exact count depends on which async observers have fired by assertion time. Use `instanceof` to filter: `findBySubjectId(id, tenancyId).stream().filter(e -> e instanceof SpecificLedgerEntry).count()`. This makes the assertion correct by construction regardless of which other entries are present.

entries.title: casehub-ledger: actor-scoped erasure receipt queries return empty after GDPR erase — tokenisation severs the link
entries.body:
casehub-ledger: actor-scoped erasure receipt queries return empty after GDPR erase — tokenisation severs the link

### Symptom
After calling `GdprErasureService.erase(actorId, tenancyId, reason)`, a JPQL query
scoped to the case's actors returns zero erasure receipts — even though receipts exist.

### Context
A compliance service builds per-case GDPR evidence. The natural approach is to extract
`actorId` values from the case's ledger entries, then query:
`SELECT e.id FROM ErasureReceiptLedgerEntry e WHERE e.tenancyId = :tenancyId AND e.erasedActorId IN :actorIds`

### What was tried (didn't work)
Scoping the erasure receipt query by the case's actor IDs. The query returns empty
because the actor IDs in the ledger entries are now opaque tokens, not the original
identity strings stored in `ErasureReceiptLedgerEntry.erasedActorId`.

### Root cause
`GdprErasureService.erase()` tokenises the actor's identity in all ledger entries —
replacing the original `actorId` with an opaque token and severing the token→identity
mapping. `ErasureReceiptLedgerEntry` stores the *original* `erasedActorId` (pre-tokenisation).
Post-erasure, `entry.actorId` (token) ≠ `receipt.erasedActorId` (original). The IN clause
matches nothing.

### Fix
Query erasure receipts by tenant only — not by actor:
```sql
SELECT e.id FROM ErasureReceiptLedgerEntry e WHERE e.tenancyId = :tenancyId
```
This is the only correct approach post-tokenisation. The compliance report answers
"has any GDPR erasure been performed in this tenant?" — it cannot answer "was this
specific case's actor erased?" because that link is intentionally destroyed.

### Why this is non-obvious
The actor-scoped query is the natural first instinct — it's more precise and avoids
false positives from unrelated erasures. It compiles, runs without error, and returns
an empty list. No exception, no warning. The silent empty result is only detectable
through test flakiness when test ordering varies.

results.query_text: JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing — subclass field shadows base class column causing Hibernate persist failure with NOT NULL constraint violation

entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement: base class NOT NULL fails despite subclass field being set
entries.body:
JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement: base class NOT NULL fails despite subclass field being set

### Symptom

`org.hibernate.PropertyValueException: not-null property references a null or transient value for entity CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId`

The test code explicitly sets `cle.tenancyId = "test-tenant"`. Java field access confirms the value is set. The error is impossible — except it isn't.

### Context

JPA JOINED inheritance with a base class (`LedgerEntry`) and subclass (`CaseLedgerEntry`). Both declare a `tenancyId` field with `@Column(name = "tenancy_id", nullable = false)`. In JOINED inheritance, these map to separate columns on separate tables (`ledger_entry.tenancy_id` and `case_ledger_entry.tenancy_id`).

### What was tried (didn't work)

1. Setting `tenancyId` on the entity before calling `save()` — still fails
2. Using `em.persist()` directly instead of the repository's `save()` — still fails
3. Setting the base field via reflection workaround — works but is wrong
4. Clean rebuild (`mvn clean`) to rule out stale bytecode — still fails

### Root cause

Java field shadowing creates two separate fields: `LedgerEntry.tenancyId` and `CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId`. They share the same name but are different fields in the JVM.

Hibernate bytecode enhancement (active by default in Quarkus 3.x) intercepts field access on entity classes. When code does `cle.tenancyId = "value"`, the enhancement transparently routes to the subclass field. When code does `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId`, the enhancement ALSO routes to the subclass field — making the shadowing invisible to Java code.

But `em.persist()` reads field values via reflection on the actual declared fields. `LedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId")` reads the base class field — which was never set (all writes were routed to the subclass by enhancement). The `nullable = false` constraint on the base table column fires.

### Diagnostic

```java
CaseLedgerEntry cle = new CaseLedgerEntry();
cle.tenancyId = "from-subclass";

// Java access (enhanced — both route to subclass):
cle.tenancyId                           → "from-subclass"
((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId           → "from-subclass"

// Reflection (raw — reveals the actual field state):
CaseLedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId").get(cle) → "from-subclass"
LedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId").get(cle)     → null  // THE BUG
```

### Fix

Remove the shadowing field from the subclass. JPA JOINED inheritance maps the base class field to the base table automatically — the subclass does not need its own declaration.

```java
// BEFORE (broken):
public class CaseLedgerEntry extends LedgerEntry {
    @Column(name = "tenancy_id", nullable = false)
    public String tenancyId;  // SHADOWS LedgerEntry.tenancyId
}

// AFTER (correct):
public class CaseLedgerEntry extends LedgerEntry {
    // tenancyId inherited from LedgerEntry — no shadowing
}
```

Migration: drop the `tenancy_id` column from the join table (it's redundant with `ledger_entry.tenancy_id`).

### Why this is non-obvious

Hibernate's bytecode enhancement makes the shadowing completely invisible to Java code. Both `cle.tenancyId` and `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId` return the same value. The error message names the subclass entity but the actual null is on the base class — and you can't see it without reflection. The symptom appears impossible: "the field is null but I literally just set it."

### Why this fix

Removing the shadowing field is the only correct fix. Workarounds (setting the base field via reflection, using a special save method) are fragile — they couple application code to JPA internals and break when the entity hierarchy changes. The field belongs on the base class; the subclass inherits it.

### Alternatives considered

- **Reflection workaround in test helpers** — sets the base field directly. Works but couples tests to the field shadowing structure and masks the underlying design bug.
- **Setting both fields explicitly** — impossible via normal Java code because enhancement intercepts the access. Would require reflection on every write path.
- **Hibernate `@Access(AccessType.PROPERTY)` with getter/setter** — might route correctly but changes the access strategy for all fields and is a much larger change.

### Caveats

- This only triggers when bytecode enhancement is active. Without enhancement (e.g. plain Hibernate outside Quarkus), both `cle.tenancyId` and `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId` access their respective declared fields — the shadowing is visible and the developer would naturally set both.
- The fix requires a Flyway migration to drop the redundant column from the join table.

**See also:** [GE-20260609-58e5d9](GE-20260609-58e5d9.md) — JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping

entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance subclass with its own repository bypasses base-class save() invariants — invisible until a constraint surfaces it
JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping — setting child field leaves parent nullable=false column null
entries.body:
JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping — setting child field leaves parent nullable=false column null

## Context

A JPA JOINED inheritance hierarchy where the child entity redeclares a field already declared in
the parent entity — both with `@Column(nullable = false)`. The child table gets a denormalized copy
of the column (e.g. for a local index to avoid joins). This pattern is intentional for performance
but creates a hidden trap for external writers.

Example from casehub platform:
- `LedgerEntry.tenancyId @Column(name="tenancy_id", nullable=false)` — in `ledger_entry` table
- `CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId @Column(name="tenancy_id", nullable=false, length=64)` — in `case_ledger_entry` table

## Symptom

```
org.hibernate.PropertyValueException: not-null property references a null or transient value
  for entity io.casehub.ledger.model.CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId
```

Thrown even though the code explicitly sets `entry.tenancyId = "tenant-1"` before calling
`em.persist()`. The value is visibly non-null; the error says it is null.

## Root cause

Java field access is statically resolved by the reference type at compile time. When `entry`
is declared as `CaseLedgerEntry`:

```java
entry.tenancyId = value;   // resolves to CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId (shadow field)
```

This writes to the child's own field (mapped to `case_ledger_entry.tenancy_id`). The parent's
field `LedgerEntry.tenancyId` (mapped to `ledger_entry.tenancy_id`, also `nullable=false`) is
never written. Hibernate's nullability check validates BOTH the child's property AND the parent's
property — the parent's property is null → `PropertyValueException`.

## Fix

Set both fields explicitly from any external code that touches the entity:

```java
entry.tenancyId = tenancyId;                    // CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId (case_ledger_entry)
((LedgerEntry) entry).tenancyId = tenancyId;    // LedgerEntry.tenancyId (ledger_entry)
```

The explicit cast forces the Java compiler to emit `PUTFIELD LedgerEntry.tenancyId` — a
separate bytecode instruction targeting the parent class's field declaration, distinct from
the child's shadow field.

The correct long-term fix: remove the field redeclaration from the child entity. Use a
`@SecondaryTable` or a Hibernate `@Formula` to maintain the index without the shadow field.

## Why this is non-obvious

The call site `entry.tenancyId = value` looks complete. The entity compiles and CDI-deploys
without warnings. The failure surfaces at `em.persist()` with a message naming the child entity
and property — which ARE set — not the parent entity and property — which are NOT set.

The Hibernate nullability check message `entity CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId` refers to the
property-path in the entity class hierarchy, not the Java field that is actually null. This
leads to the investigation going in the wrong direction (inspecting the child's field assignment
rather than the parent's).

Any code that uses direct field access from outside the entity (common in JPA entities without
getters/setters) is silently broken when this pattern is in play. Only tests that actually
perform a database persist — not those that simply assign and read back — will catch it.

**See also:** [GE-20260612-1f4ed8](GE-20260612-1f4ed8.md) — JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement causes NOT NULL failure

entries.title: JPA InheritanceType.JOINED forces all hierarchy entities into one persistence unit
entries.body:
JPA InheritanceType.JOINED forces all hierarchy entities into one persistence unit

## Symptom

Migrating to named Hibernate persistence units fails with an error about missing entities or a persistence unit resolution error, even though the entities are correctly annotated. The issue only appears when entities from two different libraries share a `JOINED` inheritance hierarchy.

## Context

Occurs when: (1) migrating from the default Hibernate PU to a named PU, (2) entity `B extends A` with `@Inheritance(strategy = InheritanceType.JOINED)`, and (3) `A` and `B` live in different Maven modules or libraries.

## Root Cause

JPA requires all entities in an inheritance hierarchy to share a single persistence unit. If `A` is in `io.lib.model` and `B` is in `io.other.entity`, the named PU `packages` config must include both packages — even if you only want the `io.other` entities in that PU. Specifying only `io.other` causes Hibernate to fail because it can't resolve the full hierarchy.

## Fix

Include both packages in the named PU configuration:

```properties
quarkus.hibernate-orm.mypu.packages=io.other.entity,io.lib.model
```

This forces the parent entity's library into the same persistence unit, which may have schema implications (both libraries' tables are created/migrated by the same Flyway run).

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The constraint is a JPA specification requirement, but the error message from Quarkus/Hibernate does not say "hierarchy entities must share a PU." It typically presents as a mapping exception or missing entity error that points to the child entity, not to the inheritance relationship. The fix — including an external library's package in your PU config — feels wrong and is not mentioned in Quarkus named datasource documentation.

**See also:** GE-20260422-f86f42 — dual-PU setup when a library injects @Default EntityManager

**See also:** [GE-20260512-4d6f48](GE-20260512-4d6f48.md) — Panache entities cannot be scanned by two Quarkus persistence units simultaneously

entries.title: Static entity factory creates JPA child entities without multi-tenancy fields — NOT NULL INSERT fails
entries.body:
Static entity factory creates JPA child entities without multi-tenancy fields — NOT NULL INSERT fails

### Symptom

```
org.hibernate.exception.ConstraintViolationException: column "tenancy_id" violates not-null constraint
```

Entities persisted via a static factory or utility method fail on INSERT even though the owning
parent entity has `tenancyId` set correctly.

### Context

`LedgerMerkleTree.append(digest, currentFrontier, subjectId)` is a pure static utility that
builds new `LedgerMerkleFrontier` nodes. It has no knowledge of tenancy context. The caller
in `ReactiveLedgerEntryJpaRepository.save()` sets `tenancyId` on the `LedgerEntry` being saved
but forgot to propagate it to the frontier nodes returned by `append()`. The nodes go into
`session.persist()` with `tenancyId = null`, violating the NOT NULL constraint.

### Root cause

Static factory and utility methods that create JPA entities operate outside any tenancy context.
Multi-tenancy fields (`tenancyId`, `tenantKey`, etc.) are caller responsibilities, not factory
responsibilities. The omission is invisible until the `session.persist()` flush fires — and only
on code paths where the NOT NULL column is actually written.

### Fix

Propagate multi-tenancy fields from the parent entity onto all child entities immediately after
the factory call, before any `session.persist()`:

```java
final List<LedgerMerkleFrontier> newFrontier =
    LedgerMerkleTree.append(e.digest, currentFrontier, e.subjectId);
newFrontier.forEach(node -> node.tenancyId = e.tenancyId);  // ← propagate before persist
// ... then session.persist(node) for each
```

### Why this is non-obvious

The parent entity (`LedgerEntry`) has `tenancyId` set correctly and was used for all prior
operations. The factory method looks complete — it takes all the structural parameters it needs.
The tenancy propagation step is easy to forget because it is not part of the factory's contract
and there is no type system or annotation to enforce it.

entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance: filtering by subclass column vs inherited column for same UUID — only inherited uses the index
em.merge() + em.remove() on JOINED inheritance entity throws OptimisticLockException from wrong EntityManager context
entries.body:
em.merge() + em.remove() on JOINED inheritance entity throws OptimisticLockException from wrong EntityManager context

## Symptom

```
jakarta.persistence.OptimisticLockException: Unexpected row count
(expected row count 1 but was 0) [delete from subclass_table where id=?]
```

Deletion succeeds for the base table row but fails for the subclass table row — or vice versa.

## Context

Occurs when:
1. Entities are loaded by one `EntityManager` (e.g., via `repo.listAll()` which uses the repo's CDI-injected EM)
2. Another `EntityManager` (e.g., the service's own `@Inject EntityManager`) calls `em.merge(detachedEntity)` then `em.remove(mergedEntity)`
3. The entity uses `@Inheritance(strategy = InheritanceType.JOINED)` with a base table and one or more subclass tables

## Root Cause

For `JOINED` inheritance, Hibernate must delete rows from **both** the base table and the subclass table, in the correct order. When `em.merge()` re-attaches a detached entity from a different EM context, Hibernate may reconstruct the entity as the base type only — losing the concrete subclass identity. `em.remove()` then issues the DELETE in the wrong order or against the wrong table, resulting in row count 0 for the subclass DELETE.

The error message ("Unexpected row count") suggests optimistic locking or concurrent modification — neither of which is the actual cause. The real issue is polymorphic type resolution on merge.

## Fix

Use `em.find()` in the **current** EntityManager context to obtain a properly typed, managed entity before calling `em.remove()`:

```java
// WRONG — entity loaded by a different EM; merge loses subclass identity
em.remove(em.contains(entry) ? entry : em.merge(entry));

// RIGHT — find in THIS EM context; Hibernate resolves the concrete subclass type
LedgerEntry managed = em.find(LedgerEntry.class, entry.id);
if (managed != null) {
    em.remove(managed);
}
```

`em.find()` issues a polymorphic `SELECT` that returns the concrete subclass type. `em.remove()` on that managed entity correctly deletes the subclass row first, then the base row.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The error message says "Unexpected row count" with a hint about optimistic locking — nothing in the message suggests cross-EM context or polymorphic type resolution. The pattern `em.contains(x) ? x : em.merge(x)` is commonly seen as the "safe" re-attachment idiom and works correctly for single-table entities. For JOINED inheritance it silently loses the subclass type.

### Why this fix

`em.find()` is guaranteed to return the correct polymorphic type because Hibernate uses the discriminator value from the base table row to select the right subclass. This is the same mechanism used when loading entities via JPQL. `em.merge()` does not make this guarantee for entities from a different persistence context.

entries.title: JPA subclass JPQL query in LedgerEntryRepository silently hides domain entries from consuming apps — IDX_LEDGER_ENTRY_SUBJECT_SEQ violation
entries.body:
JPA subclass JPQL query in LedgerEntryRepository silently hides domain entries from consuming apps — IDX_LEDGER_ENTRY_SUBJECT_SEQ violation

### What happened

`AmlInvestigationResourceTest` and `AmlLedgerChainTest` failed with:

```
Unique index or primary key violation: "IDX_LEDGER_ENTRY_SUBJECT_SEQ ON
LEDGER_ENTRY(SUBJECT_ID, SEQUENCE_NUMBER) VALUES (UUID '...', 1)"
insert into ledger_entry (..., dtype, id) values (..., 'QHORUS_MESSAGE', ?)
```

The qhorus COMMAND dispatch was trying to write `sequenceNumber=1` for a subject (`caseId`) that already had an `AmlCaseOpenedLedgerEntry` at `sequenceNumber=1`. Investigation showed that qhorus's `LedgerWriteService.record()` computed the next sequence number via:

```java
final int sequenceNumber = repository.findLatestBySubjectId(resolvedSubjectId)
        .map(e -> e.sequenceNumber + 1).orElse(1);
```

### Root cause

`repository` was `MessageLedgerEntryRepository`, which implemented `LedgerEntryRepository` with a **subclass-scoped** JPQL query:

```java
// WRONG — only sees QHORUS_MESSAGE dtype rows
"SELECT e FROM MessageLedgerEntry e WHERE e.subjectId = :sid ORDER BY e.sequenceNumber DESC"
```

With JOINED inheritance, `FROM MessageLedgerEntry` only returns rows with `dtype='QHORUS_MESSAGE'`. The `AmlCaseOpenedLedgerEntry` committed earlier (dtype=`AML_CASE_OPENED`) was invisible to this query — so `findLatestBySubjectId` returned `Optional.empty()` and assigned `sequenceNumber=1`, colliding with the domain entry.

The `IDX_LEDGER_ENTRY_SUBJECT_SEQ` constraint is on the base `LEDGER_ENTRY` table — it enforces global uniqueness across ALL subtypes for a given subject. The sequence query must be equally global.

### Fix

Changed to base-class JPQL so all subtypes are visible:

```java
// CORRECT — sees all LedgerEntry subtypes for the subject
"SELECT e FROM LedgerEntry e WHERE e.subjectId = :sid ORDER BY e.sequenceNumber DESC"
```

The proper re-architecture (casehub-qhorus#253) introduced a dedicated `LedgerEntryJpaRepository` using `FROM LedgerEntry` throughout, and `MessageLedgerEntryRepository` no longer implements `LedgerEntryRepository` — it retains only qhorus-scoped queries.

### Why this is non-obvious

qhorus's own tests never mix non-qhorus `LedgerEntry` subtypes in the same subject, so the scoped query always looked correct in qhorus CI. The bug only surfaces in a consuming application that writes both domain entries (`AmlCaseOpenedLedgerEntry`) and dispatches qhorus messages to the same subject (`caseId`). The symptom (unique constraint on sequence number) looks like a race condition, but the actual cause is a dtype-scope bug in the sequence-assignment query.

### Constraints

Applies when: (1) multiple teams write different `LedgerEntry` subclasses to the same `subjectId`, AND (2) the sequence-assignment query is scoped to one subclass. Any `LedgerEntryRepository` implementation that narrows JPQL to a subclass is potentially broken for consumers that share subjects across dtypes.

### Why this fix

A dedicated `LedgerEntryJpaRepository` (separate from `MessageLedgerEntryRepository`) enforces at the type level that cross-dtype operations use the correct base-class query. Patching the subclass JPQL directly would not prevent future regressions when new repositories are added.

entries.title: Fire CDI Event<T> from registry writes to decouple provisioners from transport implementations
Canonical CloudEvent adapter pattern — 7 rules for CDI async adapters
CDI @Observes and @ObservesAsync are separate delivery channels — @Observes never receives fireAsync() events
Await CDI fireAsync() delivery in @ConsumeEvent reactive chain via Uni.createFrom().completionStage()
entries.body:
Await CDI fireAsync() delivery in @ConsumeEvent reactive chain via Uni.createFrom().completionStage()

## Await CDI fireAsync() delivery in @ConsumeEvent reactive chain

### Context

A Quarkus @ConsumeEvent handler returns Uni<Void>. Inside the chain, lifecycleEvents.fireAsync(event)
is called inside .invoke(). The CompletionStage is discarded. @ObservesAsync observers may run AFTER
the handler's Uni completes.

### The technique

Split the terminal .invoke() into two steps:

```java
// Before — CompletionStage discarded
.invoke(() -> {
    eventBus.publish(addr, payload);
    lifecycleEvents.fireAsync(event); // discarded
});

// After — CDI delivery awaited in .chain()
.invoke(() -> {
    eventBus.publish(addr, payload); // still fire-and-forget
})
.chain(() ->
    Uni.createFrom()
        .completionStage(() -> lifecycleEvents.fireAsync(event))
        .onFailure()
        .recoverWithItem(t -> { LOG.warnf(t, "observer failed"); return null; })
        .replaceWithVoid())
```

The handler's Uni does not complete until all @ObservesAsync observers have run.
onFailure().recoverWithItem() prevents observer exceptions from breaking the handler.

### Why this is non-obvious

.invoke() reads as a side-effect callback but is fire-and-forget for CompletionStage —
Mutiny does not chain off it. Moving to .chain() with completionStage() makes CDI
delivery part of the reactive pipeline.

Use the Supplier form completionStage(() -> fireAsync(...)) — lazy, ensures fireAsync()
is called at subscription time (correct thread context), not at chain construction time.

**See also:** [GE-20260517-f31786](GE-20260517-f31786.md) — fireAsync() returns CompletionStage<Event<T>> not CompletionStage<Void> — Mutiny bridge needs .replaceWith() | [GE-20260512-0fe012](GE-20260512-0fe012.md) — CDI fireAsync() inside @Transactional dispatches immediately

**See also:** [GE-20260518-a61d1b](GE-20260518-a61d1b.md) — @ConsumeEvent(blocking=true) and @Transactional on the same method work correctly

**See also:** [GE-20260616-6f1743](GE-20260616-6f1743.md) — AtomicReference to capture values across Mutiny Uni chain when chain switches threads

entries.title: `event.fireAsync()` returns `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` not `CompletionStage<Void>` — Mutiny bridge needs `.replaceWith()`
entries.body:
`event.fireAsync()` returns `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` not `CompletionStage<Void>` — Mutiny bridge needs `.replaceWith()`

### Symptom

Type mismatch when bridging `Event.fireAsync()` to a Mutiny `Uni` pipeline:

```java
// Fails — CompletionStage<Event<MyEvent>>, not CompletionStage<VerificationResult>
return Uni.createFrom().completionStage(
    () -> suspectEvent.fireAsync(new MyEvent(...)))
    // compiler error: cannot convert Uni<Event<MyEvent>> to Uni<VerificationResult>
```

### Context

Quarkus reactive service using CDI `Event<T>` needs to fire an async event and return a `Uni<SomeType>` from the same pipeline.

### Root cause

`Event.fireAsync()` is declared in the CDI spec as returning `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` — it returns the event object itself wrapped in a completion stage, not `CompletionStage<Void>`. Most usage examples ignore the return value entirely, hiding the actual type.

### Fix

Use `.replaceWith()` to discard the `Event<T>` wrapper and substitute the intended result:

```java
return Uni.createFrom().completionStage(
    () -> suspectEvent.fireAsync(new MyEvent(entryId, actorId, keyRef)))
    .replaceWith(VerificationResult.SUSPECT);
```

### Why this is non-obvious

CDI spec says "returns a CompletionStage" without emphasising the `<Event<T>>` type parameter. Developers writing async event firing naturally assume `CompletionStage<Void>` because they're triggering a side effect and discarding the event payload. The full generic type is only visible in the CDI API Javadoc — not in any tutorials or common usage examples.

**See also:** [GE-20260423-daef97](GE-20260423-daef97.md) — CDI event.fire() does not deliver to @ObservesAsync observers — fireAsync() required

**See also:** [GE-20260529-e43076](GE-20260529-e43076.md) — await CDI fireAsync() delivery in @ConsumeEvent reactive chain

entries.title: CDI Event.fireAsync().toCompletableFuture().join() waits until all @ObservesAsync handlers commit
Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods
entries.body:
Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods

## Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods

### Symptom

A `@Transactional` method fires a CDI event and also calls an internal dispatch (channel write, database write, queue push). When the internal dispatch throws — rate-limit rejection, policy violation, missing resource — the CDI event silently never fires. The external delivery path is dead with no error.

### Context

Any method that must make two independent deliveries inside a `@Transactional` boundary:
one via CDI `fireAsync()` (external observers, notification systems) and one via an internal
service call. The pattern appears in watchdog alerting, domain event publishing, and audit
side-channels.

### Root cause

Ordering. If `fireAsync()` is placed *after* the internal dispatch and dispatch throws, the
stack unwinds before `fireAsync()` is reached. The two paths are not independent — external
delivery is silently contingent on internal dispatch succeeding.

### Fix

Place `fireAsync()` as the first statement inside the method, before the internal dispatch:

```java
private void fireAlert(Watchdog w, String summary, AlertContext context, Instant now) {
    // External delivery FIRST — not contingent on internal channel success.
    // A rate-limited, paused, or policy-rejected dispatch must not suppress this.
    alertEvents.fireAsync(new WatchdogAlertEvent(
            w.id, w.targetName, w.notificationChannel, summary, now, context));

// Internal dispatch SECOND — failure does not suppress the event already fired.
    Optional<Channel> ch = channelService.findByName(w.notificationChannel);
    if (ch.isEmpty()) {
        return;
    }
    messageService.dispatch(MessageDispatch.builder()
            .channelId(ch.get().id)
            .sender("system:watchdog")
            .type(MessageType.STATUS)
            .content(summary)
            .build());
}
```

The CDI event fires unconditionally (after the guard checks). The internal dispatch is the
conditional secondary path.

### Accepted trade-off

If the outer `@Transactional` rolls back after `fireAsync()` dispatches, the observer runs
for a state change that never committed — a ghost notification. The window is narrow in
practice (only a post-dispatch interceptor or infrastructure failure causes an outer rollback).
For alerting systems: accepted — false-positive alert is better than missed alert. For audit
or ledger systems: not acceptable — use a transactional outbox pattern instead.

### Why this is non-obvious

CDI events feel like side effects. By convention, developers put them at the *end* of a method,
after "the main work" is done. When the event IS the primary delivery path — not a side effect
— this ordering must invert. The distinction only surfaces when the two paths need to be
genuinely independent rather than sequential. Without this insight, a failing internal dispatch
silently kills external delivery with no error or log entry.

**See also:** [GE-20260512-0fe012](GE-20260512-0fe012.md) — fireAsync() dispatches immediately inside @Transactional (the timing gotcha this technique builds on)

entries.title: Quarkus Flow `listen` task does not receive from SmallRye in-memory channels
entries.body:
Quarkus Flow `listen` task does not receive from SmallRye in-memory channels

## Quarkus Flow `listen` task does not receive from SmallRye in-memory channels

**ID:** GE-0070
**Stack:** `io.quarkiverse.flow:quarkus-flow:0.7.1`, Quarkus 3.34.2, SmallRye Reactive Messaging
**Symptom:** A `Flow` subclass with `listen("tick", toOne("org.acme.example.tick"))` in its `descriptor()` never fires when a `MutinyEmitter<MyType>` emits on a SmallRye in-memory channel. No error is thrown. The workflow is silently idle.
**Context:** Attempting to wire a SmallRye `@Channel("my-channel") MutinyEmitter<MyType>` to a Quarkus Flow workflow using the FuncDSL `listen` task.

### Root cause

The `listen` task in Quarkus Flow (CNCF Serverless Workflow spec) subscribes to CloudEvents, not to SmallRye Reactive Messaging channels. The `toOne("org.acme.example.tick")` string is a CloudEvent type, not a channel name. SmallRye in-memory channels carry plain Java objects — they are not CloudEvents and cannot be received by the `listen` task without additional CloudEvent wrapping infrastructure.

### Fix

Bridge SmallRye to the Flow engine using `@Incoming` + `startInstance()` directly on the `Flow` subclass. See GE-0071.

```java
// BROKEN — listen() doesn't receive from SmallRye in-memory @Channel
@Override
public Workflow descriptor() {
    return workflow("my-flow")
        .tasks(
            listen("tick", toOne("org.acme.example.tick")),  // never fires
            ...
        ).build();
}
```

### Why non-obvious

The quarkus-flow documentation and FuncDSL skill show `listen` as a standard reactive task. Nothing indicates it requires CloudEvent format specifically. The failure is silent — no error, no log, the flow just never starts. Developers naturally try the `listen` task first when integrating with SmallRye messaging.

*Score: 13/15 · Included because: silent failure, non-obvious CloudEvent requirement, affects the primary integration pattern developers will attempt · Reservation: only verified on 0.7.1 with in-memory connector*

**See also:** GE-20260414-5b3897 — quarkus-flow uses the CNCF Serverless Workflow SDK directly — not Kogito

EndpointCapability.RECEIVE is wrong for poll streams — use QUERY
entries.body:
EndpointCapability.RECEIVE is wrong for poll streams — use QUERY

## Symptom

`EndpointRegistry.discover()` filtered by `EndpointCapability.RECEIVE` returns poll target endpoints alongside Kafka topics and webhook receivers. Filtering by `EndpointProtocol.HTTP` alone does not distinguish poll targets from webhook inbound receivers. The wrong module picks up endpoints it should not handle.

## Context

When registering stream endpoint descriptors — Kafka topics, AMQP queues, webhooks, poll targets — all feel like "receiving data." The natural choice for all five is `EndpointCapability.RECEIVE`. But poll uses a different access pattern from the others.

## Root cause

`EndpointCapability.RECEIVE` means "Endpoint can push data to the caller (webhook delivery, Kafka consume, SSE)." This is correct for:
- Kafka — the broker pushes messages to our consumer
- AMQP — same push semantics
- Webhook — external system POSTs to our endpoint
- Camel — route delivers to CDI observer

But **poll** (platform-streams-poll) initiates an HTTP GET — we pull from the endpoint. That is `QUERY`: "Caller can issue a read request and receive a synchronous response (HTTP GET, MCP query)."

Both poll and webhook use `EndpointProtocol.HTTP`. Without the correct capability, there is no way to distinguish them in `EndpointRegistry.discover()` queries.

## Fix

Use `EndpointCapability.QUERY` for poll targets:

```java
// WRONG — poll target registered as RECEIVE
EndpointDescriptor wrong = new EndpointDescriptor(
    path, tenancyId, EndpointType.SYSTEM, EndpointProtocol.HTTP,
    Map.of(...), null,
    Set.of(EndpointCapability.RECEIVE));  // incorrect for pull/GET

// CORRECT — poll target registered as QUERY
EndpointDescriptor correct = new EndpointDescriptor(
    path, tenancyId, EndpointType.SYSTEM, EndpointProtocol.HTTP,
    Map.of(...), null,
    Set.of(EndpointCapability.QUERY));   // HTTP GET initiated by us
```

The poll module discovers endpoints with `EndpointCapability.QUERY`; webhook registers with `EndpointCapability.RECEIVE`. Both use `HTTP` protocol — capability is the only discriminator.

## Why this is non-obvious

"Receiving data" semantically covers both patterns. The distinction ("who initiates?") only surfaces when you read the Javadoc carefully: RECEIVE = endpoint pushes to caller; QUERY = caller issues a read request. The failure mode is silent — wrong module discovery, no error, no warning.

**See also:** [GE-20260615-d008ea](GE-20260615-d008ea.md) — EndpointPropertyKeys.URL does not apply to KAFKA — only HTTP, GRPC, MCP, CAMEL, QHORUS

entries.title: CDI event.fire() does not deliver to @ObservesAsync observers — fireAsync() required separately
Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods
entries.body:
Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods

## Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods

### Symptom

A `@Transactional` method fires a CDI event and also calls an internal dispatch (channel write, database write, queue push). When the internal dispatch throws — rate-limit rejection, policy violation, missing resource — the CDI event silently never fires. The external delivery path is dead with no error.

### Context

Any method that must make two independent deliveries inside a `@Transactional` boundary:
one via CDI `fireAsync()` (external observers, notification systems) and one via an internal
service call. The pattern appears in watchdog alerting, domain event publishing, and audit
side-channels.

### Root cause

Ordering. If `fireAsync()` is placed *after* the internal dispatch and dispatch throws, the
stack unwinds before `fireAsync()` is reached. The two paths are not independent — external
delivery is silently contingent on internal dispatch succeeding.

### Fix

Place `fireAsync()` as the first statement inside the method, before the internal dispatch:

```java
private void fireAlert(Watchdog w, String summary, AlertContext context, Instant now) {
    // External delivery FIRST — not contingent on internal channel success.
    // A rate-limited, paused, or policy-rejected dispatch must not suppress this.
    alertEvents.fireAsync(new WatchdogAlertEvent(
            w.id, w.targetName, w.notificationChannel, summary, now, context));

// Internal dispatch SECOND — failure does not suppress the event already fired.
    Optional<Channel> ch = channelService.findByName(w.notificationChannel);
    if (ch.isEmpty()) {
        return;
    }
    messageService.dispatch(MessageDispatch.builder()
            .channelId(ch.get().id)
            .sender("system:watchdog")
            .type(MessageType.STATUS)
            .content(summary)
            .build());
}
```

The CDI event fires unconditionally (after the guard checks). The internal dispatch is the
conditional secondary path.

### Accepted trade-off

If the outer `@Transactional` rolls back after `fireAsync()` dispatches, the observer runs
for a state change that never committed — a ghost notification. The window is narrow in
practice (only a post-dispatch interceptor or infrastructure failure causes an outer rollback).
For alerting systems: accepted — false-positive alert is better than missed alert. For audit
or ledger systems: not acceptable — use a transactional outbox pattern instead.

### Why this is non-obvious

CDI events feel like side effects. By convention, developers put them at the *end* of a method,
after "the main work" is done. When the event IS the primary delivery path — not a side effect
— this ordering must invert. The distinction only surfaces when the two paths need to be
genuinely independent rather than sequential. Without this insight, a failing internal dispatch
silently kills external delivery with no error or log entry.

**See also:** [GE-20260512-0fe012](GE-20260512-0fe012.md) — fireAsync() dispatches immediately inside @Transactional (the timing gotcha this technique builds on)

entries.title: `event.fireAsync()` returns `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` not `CompletionStage<Void>` — Mutiny bridge needs `.replaceWith()`
entries.body:
`event.fireAsync()` returns `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` not `CompletionStage<Void>` — Mutiny bridge needs `.replaceWith()`

### Symptom

Type mismatch when bridging `Event.fireAsync()` to a Mutiny `Uni` pipeline:

```java
// Fails — CompletionStage<Event<MyEvent>>, not CompletionStage<VerificationResult>
return Uni.createFrom().completionStage(
    () -> suspectEvent.fireAsync(new MyEvent(...)))
    // compiler error: cannot convert Uni<Event<MyEvent>> to Uni<VerificationResult>
```

### Context

Quarkus reactive service using CDI `Event<T>` needs to fire an async event and return a `Uni<SomeType>` from the same pipeline.

### Root cause

`Event.fireAsync()` is declared in the CDI spec as returning `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` — it returns the event object itself wrapped in a completion stage, not `CompletionStage<Void>`. Most usage examples ignore the return value entirely, hiding the actual type.

### Fix

Use `.replaceWith()` to discard the `Event<T>` wrapper and substitute the intended result:

```java
return Uni.createFrom().completionStage(
    () -> suspectEvent.fireAsync(new MyEvent(entryId, actorId, keyRef)))
    .replaceWith(VerificationResult.SUSPECT);
```

### Why this is non-obvious

CDI spec says "returns a CompletionStage" without emphasising the `<Event<T>>` type parameter. Developers writing async event firing naturally assume `CompletionStage<Void>` because they're triggering a side effect and discarding the event payload. The full generic type is only visible in the CDI API Javadoc — not in any tutorials or common usage examples.

**See also:** [GE-20260423-daef97](GE-20260423-daef97.md) — CDI event.fire() does not deliver to @ObservesAsync observers — fireAsync() required

**See also:** [GE-20260529-e43076](GE-20260529-e43076.md) — await CDI fireAsync() delivery in @ConsumeEvent reactive chain

entries.title: langchain4j-agentic DeclarativeUtil has a single CDI resolver hook for @ChatModelSupplier only — no equivalent for other supplier types
entries.body:
langchain4j-agentic DeclarativeUtil has a single CDI resolver hook for @ChatModelSupplier only — no equivalent for other supplier types

### What it is
`DeclarativeUtil` in `langchain4j-agentic` exposes a single static list `chatSupplierParameterResolvers` with one registration method `addChatSupplierParameterResolver(ChatSupplierParameterResolver)`. This resolver hook fires only during `@ChatModelSupplier` and `@StreamingChatModelSupplier` processing. All other supplier annotations (`@ToolsSupplier`, `@ChatMemorySupplier`, `@ChatMemoryProviderSupplier`, `@ContentRetrieverSupplier`, `@RetrievalAugmentorSupplier`, `@AgentListenerSupplier`, `@ToolProviderSupplier`) call `checkArguments(method)` which enforces **zero parameters** before invoking the static method directly — no resolver hook exists for these paths.

### Why this matters for framework integrators
Any DI framework (Quarkus, Spring, Guice) wanting to inject CDI/Spring beans into supplier methods is blocked for all supplier types except chat model suppliers. The `@CdiBean` workaround in `quarkus-langchain4j` only works for `@ChatModelSupplier` as a result. Users who need a CDI-managed `ContentRetriever` or `ChatMemory` must use `Arc.container()` or `ApplicationContext.getBean()` inside the static method body — bypassing injection, scope management, and testability.

### How to discover this
Only by decompiling `langchain4j-agentic-1.15.1-beta25.jar` and reading `DeclarativeUtil.class`. The library has no published Javadoc for internal classes and no documentation of the SPI surface area.

```bash
# Locate the JAR
find ~/.m2/repository/dev/langchain4j/langchain4j-agentic -name "*.jar" | grep -v sources | head -1

# The class structure reveals: addChatSupplierParameterResolver() is the only hook
# All other configureAgent() branches call checkArguments() then invokeStatic()
```

### Workaround
For Quarkus: use `SyntheticCreationalContext.getInjectedReference()` in the synthetic bean creator (`AgenticRecorder`) to inject CDI beans at agent construction time, bypassing the static supplier method entirely. This requires detecting at build time that no static supplier method exists for a given type, then adding a CDI injection point for that type.

### Upstream path
An upstream PR to `langchain4j-agentic` should propose a general `SupplierParameterResolver` SPI (not chat-model-specific) and add resolver hooks to all supplier type branches in `configureAgent()`. Frame as a DI-framework-agnostic improvement — Spring users would benefit from the same hook.

### Why this is non-obvious
The library name suggests platform independence (`langchain4j` not `langchain4j-spring`), so the assumption is that all supplier types are equally extensible. The asymmetry between `@ChatModelSupplier` (has a resolver hook) and all other supplier annotations (do not) is not mentioned in any README, Javadoc, or issue tracker. The class is package-private in the library with no stable API guarantee.

entries.title: quarkus-langchain4j Ollama extension registers @Default ChatModel — clashes with @DefaultBean fallback
entries.body:
quarkus-langchain4j Ollama extension registers @Default ChatModel — clashes with @DefaultBean fallback

## Symptom

When the quarkus-langchain4j-ollama extension is on the classpath alongside a
`@DefaultBean ChatModel` implementation, Quarkus throws at startup:

```
AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type ChatModel and qualifiers [@Default]
  - CLASS bean [... AgentProviderChatModel] qualifiers=[@Any, @Default]
  - SYNTHETIC bean [... OllamaChatModel] qualifiers=[@Any, @Default]
```

## Context

A custom `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped ChatModel` implementation exists to serve as a
fallback (e.g. a Claude CLI bridge). The expectation is that adding the Ollama extension
places its `ChatModel` higher-priority, which should silence the `@DefaultBean`. Instead,
both are registered with `@Default` and Quarkus cannot choose between them.

## Root cause

`@DefaultBean` in Quarkus ARC means: "this bean is only active if no other `@Default`-
qualified bean of the same type is present." The mechanism works when the competing bean
is `@ApplicationScoped` with no explicit qualifier (which implicitly gets `@Default`), or
when the competing bean is annotated with `@Alternative @Priority(N)`. However, the
quarkus-langchain4j-ollama extension registers its model as a **SYNTHETIC bean with an
explicit `@Default` qualifier** — not as a higher-priority alternative. ARC sees two
explicit `@Default` beans and cannot resolve.

## Fix

Veto the `@DefaultBean` explicitly when the Ollama extension is active by pairing
`@IfBuildProperty` with `-Dcasehub.eval.claude-provider.enabled=false` at build time:

```java
@DefaultBean
@ApplicationScoped
@IfBuildProperty(name = "casehub.eval.claude-provider.enabled",
                 stringValue = "true", enableIfMissing = true)
class AgentProviderChatModel implements ChatModel { ... }
```

```bash
# Run with Ollama — veto the DefaultBean
mvn clean test -Peval,eval-ollama \
  -Dcasehub.eval.claude-provider.enabled=false \
  ...
```

`mvn clean` is required to force fresh augmentation when toggling this property (see
related entry on augmentation caching).

## Why this is non-obvious

`@DefaultBean` is marketed as "the bean that loses to any other bean of the same type."
In practice it only loses to beans that don't also carry an explicit `@Default` qualifier.
Quarkus LangChain4j provider extensions register SYNTHETIC beans with explicit `@Default`
rather than `@Alternative @Priority`, making the clash non-obvious. The `@DefaultBean`
javadoc does not describe this failure mode.

**See also:** [GE-20260601-fcf0d9](GE-20260601-fcf0d9.md) — two @DefaultBean beans for the same type → AmbiguousResolutionException | [GE-20260531-1ec900](GE-20260531-1ec900.md) — Quarkus ARC registers non-CDI abstract class as @Dependent bean

SPLADE (MS MARCO) has zero Java domain vocabulary — expands ChatModel to hotel/beauty/renovation
entries.body:
SPLADE (MS MARCO) has zero Java domain vocabulary — expands ChatModel to hotel/beauty/renovation

### Symptom
Adding SPLADE sparse embeddings to dense retrieval (RRF fusion) churns result sets without improving quality for Java/CDI domain queries. All 28 queries changed, 3 highly relevant entries were displaced while 40 noise entries were removed — net effect ambiguous.

### Context
Hortora engine hybrid search benchmark (#28). SPLADE (`Splade_PP_en_v1`) added as sparse embedding alongside nomic-embed-text dense vectors. RRF fusion (k=60, denseTopK=40, sparseTopK=40). Tested across 14 real-world scenarios covering Java class names, CDI annotations, JPA patterns, and Quarkus framework terms.

SPLADE vocabulary analysis decoded the activated tokens for each query:

| Query | Top SPLADE Expansions | Expected (Java domain) |
|-------|----------------------|----------------------|
| `DefaultBean\|AmbiguousResolutionException` | ambiguity, groups, unclear | cdi, qualifier, alternative, inject |
| `ChatModel\|AgentSession\|LangChain4j` | agent, talk, beauty, renovation, genre | model, inference, provider, adapter |
| `Priority(100)\|CDI priority` | priorities, cds (music), urgency, precedence | qualifier, scope, alternative, bean |

Zero Tier 1 domain term activations across all 14 KW queries. Not one activation of `panache`, `jandex`, `qualifier`, `interceptor`, `singleton`, `persist`, or any unambiguous Java/CDI term.

### Root cause
Two compounding failures:

1. **BERT WordPiece tokenizer** fragments Java class names into meaningless subwords: `AmbiguousResolutionException` → `["am", "##bi", "##guous", "##reso", "##lution", "##exception"]`. The semantic content is destroyed before SPLADE runs.

2. **MS MARCO training data** contains no Java ecosystem content. SPLADE's learned vocabulary expansion maps "chat" to web-domain associations (hotels, beauty, renovation) — not to software engineering concepts.

This is expected behaviour for this model architecture, not a defect. The same limitation applies to nomic-embed-text (see GE-20260627-4712de) — the difference is that SPLADE actively ADDS noise through irrelevant expansions, whereas nomic-embed-text merely fails to discriminate.

### Fix
Do not use general-purpose (MS MARCO-trained) SPLADE models for Java/CDI domain retrieval. Three alternatives:

1. **BM25 keyword matching** (casehubio/neural-text#47/#48) — exact token matching via Qdrant full-text index. Directly addresses the vocabulary gap.
2. **Code-domain SPLADE model** (casehubio/neural-text#49) — if one exists with Java training data.
3. **Code-domain dense embedding model** (casehubio/neural-text#49) — models like CodeBERT, UniXcoder trained on source code.

### Why this is non-obvious
SPLADE is presented as a vocabulary expansion technique that recovers keyword precision lost by dense embeddings. The expectation is that it bridges the gap between semantic search and keyword matching. For general-domain web queries, this is true. For Java class names, it actively makes things worse by introducing irrelevant web-domain associations into the sparse vector.

### Why this fix
BM25 is the highest-leverage fix because it directly solves the failure mode (exact token matching for Java identifiers) without requiring model training or fine-tuning. Code-domain models are the long-term architectural fix but have higher uncertainty and longer timeline.

entries.title: quarkus-langchain4j-ollama added via Maven test-scope profile requires mvn clean to invalidate augmentation cache
entries.body:
quarkus-langchain4j-ollama added via Maven test-scope profile requires mvn clean to invalidate augmentation cache

## Symptom

After adding `quarkus-langchain4j-ollama` as a test-scope dependency inside a Maven profile
(`-Peval-ollama`), the Ollama `ChatModel` bean is not registered. Config keys are logged as
`Unrecognized configuration key "quarkus.langchain4j.ollama.chat-model.model-name"`, and the
test fails with `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` for `ChatModel`.

## Context

The module was previously built without the Ollama extension. Maven resolves the Ollama JAR
when the profile is active, but Quarkus reuses its disk-based augmentation cache from the
prior build — before the Ollama extension was on the classpath. The Ollama extension is
silently absent from augmentation: no beans registered, no config processors active.

## What was tried (didn't work)

- Running `mvn test -Peval,eval-ollama` after the JAR appeared in `~/.m2` — stale augmentation
  cache persisted, config keys still unrecognized.
- Adding `-Dquarkus.test.profile=eval` explicitly — no effect on cache.

## Root cause

Quarkus augmentation is disk-cached in `target/quarkus-app/`. When classpath changes occur
(new extension added via Maven profile), the cache invalidation heuristic does not always
detect the change, especially when the Ollama JAR was already present in `~/.m2` from a
prior `mvn dependency:resolve` call (updating file timestamps). The cache is reused, and
the Ollama extension's CDI beans are never registered.

## Fix

Run `mvn clean` before the first test run with the new profile:

```bash
JAVA_HOME=$(/usr/libexec/java_home -v 26) mvn clean test -pl eval -Peval,eval-ollama \
  -Dtest=PromptEvalTest#evaluateRealWorldScenarios \
  -Dcasehub.eval.claude-provider.enabled=false \
  "-Dquarkus.langchain4j.ollama.chat-model.model-name=qwen3:8b" \
  "-Dquarkus.langchain4j.ollama.chat-model.format=json" \
  "-Dquarkus.langchain4j.ollama.timeout=300s"
```

Subsequent runs without `clean` work correctly — the Ollama-aware augmentation is now cached.

## Why this is non-obvious

The "unrecognized configuration key" warning does not hint at augmentation caching — it reads
as though the extension was not added at all, not that a stale build artifact is in play.
The Ollama JAR is visible in `mvn dependency:resolve` output, making the missing registration
doubly confusing. The standard fix for missing CDI beans (`check your imports, check your
annotations`) leads nowhere.

**See also:** [GE-20260530-0178fd](GE-20260530-0178fd.md) — Quarkus @QuarkusTest augmentation requires extension deployment JARs in ~/.m2 | [GE-20260614-337397](GE-20260614-337397.md) — quarkus-langchain4j Ollama extension @Default ChatModel clashes with @DefaultBean
