entries.body:
langchain4j-agentic DeclarativeUtil has a single CDI resolver hook for @ChatModelSupplier only — no equivalent for other supplier types

### What it is
`DeclarativeUtil` in `langchain4j-agentic` exposes a single static list `chatSupplierParameterResolvers` with one registration method `addChatSupplierParameterResolver(ChatSupplierParameterResolver)`. This resolver hook fires only during `@ChatModelSupplier` and `@StreamingChatModelSupplier` processing. All other supplier annotations (`@ToolsSupplier`, `@ChatMemorySupplier`, `@ChatMemoryProviderSupplier`, `@ContentRetrieverSupplier`, `@RetrievalAugmentorSupplier`, `@AgentListenerSupplier`, `@ToolProviderSupplier`) call `checkArguments(method)` which enforces **zero parameters** before invoking the static method directly — no resolver hook exists for these paths.

### Why this matters for framework integrators
Any DI framework (Quarkus, Spring, Guice) wanting to inject CDI/Spring beans into supplier methods is blocked for all supplier types except chat model suppliers. The `@CdiBean` workaround in `quarkus-langchain4j` only works for `@ChatModelSupplier` as a result. Users who need a CDI-managed `ContentRetriever` or `ChatMemory` must use `Arc.container()` or `ApplicationContext.getBean()` inside the static method body — bypassing injection, scope management, and testability.

### How to discover this
Only by decompiling `langchain4j-agentic-1.15.1-beta25.jar` and reading `DeclarativeUtil.class`. The library has no published Javadoc for internal classes and no documentation of the SPI surface area.

```bash
# Locate the JAR
find ~/.m2/repository/dev/langchain4j/langchain4j-agentic -name "*.jar" | grep -v sources | head -1

# The class structure reveals: addChatSupplierParameterResolver() is the only hook
# All other configureAgent() branches call checkArguments() then invokeStatic()
```

### Workaround
For Quarkus: use `SyntheticCreationalContext.getInjectedReference()` in the synthetic bean creator (`AgenticRecorder`) to inject CDI beans at agent construction time, bypassing the static supplier method entirely. This requires detecting at build time that no static supplier method exists for a given type, then adding a CDI injection point for that type.

### Upstream path
An upstream PR to `langchain4j-agentic` should propose a general `SupplierParameterResolver` SPI (not chat-model-specific) and add resolver hooks to all supplier type branches in `configureAgent()`. Frame as a DI-framework-agnostic improvement — Spring users would benefit from the same hook.

### Why this is non-obvious
The library name suggests platform independence (`langchain4j` not `langchain4j-spring`), so the assumption is that all supplier types are equally extensible. The asymmetry between `@ChatModelSupplier` (has a resolver hook) and all other supplier annotations (do not) is not mentioned in any README, Javadoc, or issue tracker. The class is package-private in the library with no stable API guarantee.

entries.title: LangChain4j Anthropic: ResponseFormat.JSON without schema throws UnsupportedFeatureException — not a degradation
entries.body:
LangChain4j Anthropic: ResponseFormat.JSON without schema throws UnsupportedFeatureException — not a degradation

## Symptom

Passing `ResponseFormat.JSON` (type only, no schema) to `ChatRequest.builder().responseFormat(...)` for an Anthropic-backed `ChatModel` throws `UnsupportedFeatureException` rather than silently degrading or prompting.

## Context

`InternalAnthropicHelper.validate(ChatRequestParameters)` is called inside `AnthropicChatModel.doChat()` before any API call is made. The validation checks:

```java
if (parameters.responseFormat() != null
        && parameters.responseFormat().type() == ResponseFormatType.JSON
        && parameters.responseFormat().jsonSchema() == null) {
    throw new UnsupportedFeatureException(...);
}
```

This means JSON mode without a schema is explicitly rejected at the LangChain4j layer — it never reaches the Anthropic API.

## Root cause

Anthropic's native API requires a JSON schema to enforce structured output. Unlike OpenAI (which supports a bare JSON mode that produces valid but unstructured JSON), Anthropic has no schema-less JSON mode. LangChain4j's Anthropic integration reflects this correctly by refusing the schemaless request rather than silently ignoring the constraint.

## Fix

Always pair `ResponseFormat.JSON` with a `JsonSchema` when using Anthropic:

```java
ResponseFormat responseFormat = ResponseFormat.builder()
    .type(ResponseFormatType.JSON)
    .jsonSchema(JsonSchema.builder()
        .name("MySchema")
        .rootElement(JsonObjectSchema.builder()
            .addStringProperty("field", "Description")
            .required("field")
            .build())
        .build())
    .build();
```

For a broad catch on provider-specific failures (including older Claude models that don't support structured output at all), wrap the `llm.chat(request)` call in `catch (Exception e)` and degrade gracefully:

```java
try {
    ChatResponse response = llm.chat(request);
    return Optional.of(parse(response.aiMessage().text()));
} catch (Exception e) {
    log.warn("Structured enrichment failed ({}), falling back to structural rendering", e.getMessage());
    return Optional.empty();
}
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The LangChain4j docs describe `ResponseFormat.JSON` as a valid type value. There is no public documentation on which providers support it with or without a schema. The `UnsupportedFeatureException` is thrown before any network call, so there is no HTTP error to debug — it looks like an in-process configuration error with no obvious connection to the Anthropic API's structured output requirements.

The `supportedCapabilities()` mechanism exists on `ChatModel` for high-level AI Services to check before using JSON schema, but `ClaudeMarkdownRenderer`-style code calling the low-level `ChatModel.chat(ChatRequest)` API bypasses that guard entirely.

### Why this fix

`ResponseFormat.JSON` with `JsonSchema` is the only valid combination for Anthropic. Adding a schema is not optional — it is the required API surface.

entries.title: LangChain4j 1.x: ChatLanguageModel renamed to ChatModel; chat() replaced by doChat() as extension point
quarkus-langchain4j-core Quarkus extension stalls @QuarkusTest augmentation when no model provider is configured
entries.body:
quarkus-langchain4j-core Quarkus extension stalls @QuarkusTest augmentation when no model provider is configured

## Symptom

`mvn test` hangs indefinitely during Quarkus augmentation when `quarkus-langchain4j-core` is a compile-scope dependency of a non-extension library module and no AI model provider is configured. No error message, no timeout — the build just blocks.

## Context

Occurs when a library module (Jandex-indexed, not a full Quarkus extension) adds `io.quarkiverse.langchain4j:quarkus-langchain4j-core` as a compile dependency to use the `EmbeddingModel` interface. During `@QuarkusTest` augmentation, the Quarkus extension activates and waits for a model provider that never arrives.

## What was tried

Adding `quarkus-langchain4j-core` as a `provided` scope — still stalls because the extension's deployment artifact is resolved transitively during augmentation.

## Root cause

`quarkus-langchain4j-core` is a Quarkus extension (has both runtime and deployment jars). When it appears on the augmentation classpath, Quarkus activates it and expects at least one model provider (OpenAI, Ollama, etc.) to be configured. Without one, augmentation stalls waiting for the CDI bean to resolve.

## Fix

Use `dev.langchain4j:langchain4j-core` (the plain Java library, **not** the Quarkus extension) as the compile dependency. This provides the `EmbeddingModel` interface for compilation without triggering Quarkus extension activation:

```xml
<dependency>
  <groupId>dev.langchain4j</groupId>
  <artifactId>langchain4j-core</artifactId>
  <version>1.13.0</version>
</dependency>
```

For CDI injection, use `Instance<EmbeddingModel>` instead of `@Inject EmbeddingModel`:

```java
@Inject
public MyBean(final Instance<EmbeddingModel> modelInstance) {
    this.model = modelInstance.isResolvable() ? modelInstance.get() : null;
}
```

When no provider is configured, `instance.isResolvable()` returns `false` gracefully. The consuming app brings `quarkus-langchain4j-openai` (or any other provider extension), which satisfies the CDI bean at their own augmentation time.

## Why this is non-obvious

The symptom is a hang with no error — not a compilation failure or a clear exception. The natural instinct is to add the Quarkus extension for its CDI integration, but the correct separation is: library module uses the plain Java API, consuming application brings the Quarkus extension.

### Why this fix

The plain Java `langchain4j-core` provides the same `EmbeddingModel` interface without triggering extension activation. `Instance<EmbeddingModel>` makes the dependency optional at CDI resolution time, so the library works both with and without a configured provider.

**See also:** GE-20260422-3242bf — Use Instance<T> for optional CDI injection — resolves gracefully when no bean exists

### Alternatives considered

- `provided` scope: still triggers augmentation because the extension's deployment artifact is resolved transitively
- Mocking `EmbeddingModel` via `@InjectMock` in every `@QuarkusTest`: works but requires all existing tests to be updated

entries.title: LangChain4j EmbeddingModel does not implement TokenCountEstimator — use character-based DocumentSplitters.recursive(int, int)
entries.body:
LangChain4j EmbeddingModel does not implement TokenCountEstimator — use character-based DocumentSplitters.recursive(int, int)

`DocumentSplitters.recursive(int, int, TokenCountEstimator)` requires a `TokenCountEstimator` as the third argument for token-based segment sizing. `EmbeddingModel` does NOT implement `TokenCountEstimator` — they are separate interface hierarchies with no inheritance relationship.

### Symptom

Design spec or implementation assumes `EmbeddingModel` can be passed as the `TokenCountEstimator` argument to `DocumentSplitters.recursive()`. Compilation fails with "incompatible types."

### Context

When building a RAG chunking pipeline, the natural assumption is: "I already have an `EmbeddingModel` that handles tokenization for embeddings — it should work as a `TokenCountEstimator` for splitting too." The naming and domain proximity reinforce this assumption.

### Root cause

`EmbeddingModel` and `TokenCountEstimator` are independent interfaces in LangChain4j. `EmbeddingModel` knows how to embed text into vectors. `TokenCountEstimator` knows how to count tokens in text. Some LLM provider implementations (e.g. `OpenAiEmbeddingModel`) implement both, but the base `EmbeddingModel` interface does not extend `TokenCountEstimator`. ONNX-based models (`OnnxEmbeddingModel`) implement only `EmbeddingModel`.

### Fix

Use the character-based 2-arg overload:

```java
DocumentSplitter splitter = DocumentSplitters.recursive(1000, 200);
```

This splits by character count (paragraph → line → sentence → word fallback chain). Character counts are approximate by nature — the imprecision vs token counts is negligible for RAG chunk sizing.

### Why this is non-obvious

The `EmbeddingModel` interface handles tokenization internally (it must tokenize text before embedding it). A developer reasonably infers it exposes this capability via `TokenCountEstimator`. The class names reinforce the assumption: "Embedding" + "Model" + "Token" + "Count" — all in the same domain. The actual type hierarchy has no inheritance link between them.

### Alternatives considered

- Implementing a custom `TokenCountEstimator` that delegates to the embedding model's internal tokenizer — rejected because ONNX embedding models don't expose their tokenizer publicly
- Using HuggingFace Tokenizers JNI directly as a `TokenCountEstimator` — possible but adds JNI complexity for marginal accuracy improvement over character-based splitting

entries.title: Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently
entries.body:
Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently

## Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently

### Symptom

Test suite activates `@Alternative @Priority(1)` InMemory stores via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives`. Panache static queries (`Entity.<Entity>listAll()`, `Entity.find(...)`) return zero results with no error, even though data was inserted through the store.

### Root cause

Panache static methods resolve directly against JPA at the persistence layer, bypassing CDI entirely. `@Alternative` implementations intercept calls made through injected CDI interfaces, not through static Panache entry points. With InMemory stores active, the JPA entity tables are empty — Panache statics query empty tables and return zero results silently.

### Fix

In test code that activates InMemory stores, never use Panache statics to query domain entities. Always go through the injected store or service layer:

```java
// Wrong — bypasses CDI alternative, queries empty JPA table
List<Channel> channels = Channel.<Channel>listAll();

// Correct — routes through the injected alternative store
List<Channel> channels = channelStore.findAll();
// or via service/tool layer
var result = tools.listChannels();
```

### Why this is non-obvious

Panache statics look like repository methods and feel like they should respect CDI alternatives — but they don't. No error is thrown, no warning is logged; the query simply returns an empty collection. The only signal is silently wrong test data.

**See also:** [GE-20260429-a79d0e](GE-20260429-a79d0e.md) — @Alternative @Priority auto-activates without quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives

**See also:** [GE-20260519-f33c66](GE-20260519-f33c66.md) — 'Reactive' service classes may use Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative doesn't intercept

**See also:** [GE-20260528-3d3847](GE-20260528-3d3847.md) — @Transactional on @BeforeAll static methods has no effect (same CDI proxy limitation)

**See also:** [GE-20260608-d223af](GE-20260608-d223af.md) — REST Assured GPath $.findAll returns null (not []) — Hamcrest not(empty()) silently passes on null

**See also:** [GE-20260609-49bd08](GE-20260609-49bd08.md) — static Panache entity calls bypass injected store tenant filtering

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives

## Symptom

Test assertions calling `workItemStore.scanAll()` return empty even though the handler log confirms `WorkItemService.create()` ran successfully. `planItem.getStatus()` is `RUNNING` (proving the handler completed), but the store shows nothing.

## Context

Two `WorkItemStore` beans on the test classpath:
- `JpaWorkItemStore @ApplicationScoped` — production bean from `casehub-work`
- `InMemoryWorkItemStore @Alternative @Priority(1)` — test replacement from `casehub-work-testing`

CDI 4.x spec says `@Priority` on an alternative activates it globally and it should win over non-alternatives. In Quarkus ARC 3.32.2 this does not hold when the alternative comes from an external dependency JAR.

## What was tried

- Adding `InMemoryWorkItemStore` to `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` alone → "Ambiguous dependencies" deployment failure (both beans active simultaneously)
- `quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.work.runtime.repository.jpa.JpaWorkItemStore` alone → no effect; `JpaWorkItemStore` still wins
- `@Inject InMemoryWorkItemStore workItemStore` (concrete type) in the test → bypasses CDI alternative selection; test clears the right store but `WorkItemService` still writes to JPA (H2), so `scanAll()` returns empty

## Root Cause

`@Alternative @Priority(1)` from a dependency JAR is not treated as overriding a non-alternative `@ApplicationScoped` bean by Quarkus ARC. CDI spec §2.6.2 requires `@Priority` alternatives to take precedence, but ARC's resolution appears to treat both as equally active candidates when neither is the local application class. The non-alternative wins or ambiguity is raised.

## Fix

Combine both in `src/test/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
# Remove the JPA impl from CDI discovery entirely
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.work.runtime.repository.jpa.JpaWorkItemStore

# Explicitly select the in-memory replacement
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.work.testing.InMemoryWorkItemStore,\
  ...other alternatives...
```

With `JpaWorkItemStore` excluded, `InMemoryWorkItemStore` is the only active `WorkItemStore` bean. Explicit `selected-alternatives` listing then activates it. Both `WorkItemService` (via `@Inject WorkItemStore`) and the test (via `@Inject InMemoryWorkItemStore`) now reference the same singleton, and `scanAll()` finds what `create()` stored.

## Why this is non-obvious

CDI 4.x spec (and Quarkus docs on alternatives) state that `@Alternative @Priority(n)` globally activates and prioritises the alternative. A developer familiar with CDI 4.x would expect `@Priority(1)` to simply work. The failure is silent — no warning is emitted, `JpaWorkItemStore` just wins quietly. The symptom (empty `scanAll()`) points toward a different root cause (session caching, test isolation) and sends debugging in the wrong direction.

**See also:** [GE-20260429-a79d0e](GE-20260429-a79d0e.md) — @Alternative @Priority auto-activates within same module | [GE-20260521-4de4f1](GE-20260521-4de4f1.md) — TestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely

**See also:** [GE-20260601-cee623](GE-20260601-cee623.md) — QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely

**See also:** [GE-20260601-848232](GE-20260601-848232.md) — exclude-types silently does nothing for beans from Jandex-indexed JARs

**See also:** [GE-20260604-2f0889](GE-20260604-2f0889.md) — @Alternative @Priority(N) stubs appear in @All List<T> alongside original bean

**See also:** [GE-20260616-d70e7e](GE-20260616-d70e7e.md) — selected-alternatives silently accepts non-@Alternative beans

entries.title: QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives — does not merge
Hibernate dirty-check flushes PanacheEntity field writes inside Panache.withSession() — even for in-memory test stores
entries.body:
Hibernate dirty-check flushes PanacheEntity field writes inside Panache.withSession() — even for in-memory test stores

### Symptom
A JPA UPDATE fires for a field write on a `PanacheEntity` subclass, even though:
- The CDI injection test confirms the in-memory store (`InMemoryReactiveChannelStore`) is selected, not the JPA store
- The entity was created with `new Channel()` — never explicitly `persist()`ed or loaded in the current session
- `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` is correctly configured

Error:
```
org.hibernate.QueryParameterException: No argument for named parameter ':tenancyId'
```

### Context
`ReactiveMessageService.dispatch()` wraps the entire dispatch in `Panache.withSession("qhorus", () -> ...)`. Inside that session scope, the in-memory store's `updateLastActivity()` does:

```java
channel.lastActivityAt = Instant.now();
```

`Channel` is a Hibernate-enhanced `PanacheEntity`. The field write is intercepted by Hibernate's dirty tracker within the active session scope and generates a JPA UPDATE at session flush.

### Root cause
Hibernate's bytecode enhancement instruments all `PanacheEntity` subclasses with dirty tracking. Any field write on an enhanced entity within an active `Panache.withSession()` scope is detected and queued for flush — regardless of whether the entity was explicitly persisted or loaded in that session. The CDI injection test proves the right bean is injected, but it does not reveal that Hibernate's session-scoped dirty tracker is intercepting field mutations on enhanced entities.

### Fix
Make the in-memory store's `updateLastActivity()` a no-op. Do not modify enhanced entity fields in-place within a `withSession()` scope:

```java
@Override
public Uni<Void> updateLastActivity(UUID channelId, String tenancyId) {
    // No-op — modifying the Hibernate-enhanced entity in-place triggers
    // a dirty-check flush when called from within Panache.withSession().
    return Uni.createFrom().voidItem();
}
```

`lastActivityAt` is set at channel creation time via `put()`; staleness tracking is irrelevant in test contexts.

### Why this is non-obvious
The CDI direct injection test is a convincing false positive: `@Inject ReactiveChannelStore` returns `InMemoryReactiveChannelStore` ✅. This proves the right bean is injected, but it does not prove that field mutations on Hibernate-enhanced entities are safe within the dispatch session scope. The JPA UPDATE is generated by Hibernate's session-level flush mechanism, not by the in-memory store calling `repo.update()` directly.

entries.title: 'Reactive' Quarkus service classes may use blocking services or Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative in-memory stores don't intercept those paths
entries.body:
'Reactive' Quarkus service classes may use blocking services or Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative in-memory stores don't intercept those paths

### What is undocumented

A service class annotated `@Alternative` and named with "Reactive" may internally use a `blockingService` field (injected blocking CDI service) for write operations and `Entity.find()` (a Panache static) for read operations. CDI `@Alternative @Priority(1)` in-memory stores only intercept at CDI injection points — Panache statics go directly to the underlying database regardless of what alternatives are selected.

### Context

In Quarkus Qhorus (casehubio/qhorus), `ReactiveQhorusMcpTools`:
- `sendMessage()` delegates internally to `blockingMessageService` → `MessageService.send()` → `messageStore.put()` (uses the `MessageStore` CDI alternative, i.e. `InMemoryMessageStore` in tests)
- `getChannelTimeline()` delegates to `blockingGetChannelTimeline()` → `Message.<Message>find(...)` (Panache static — queries H2 directly, bypasses `MessageStore` entirely)

Result: messages written by `sendMessage()` land in `InMemoryMessageStore` (in-memory). Messages read by `getChannelTimeline()` come from H2 (empty in tests). The two paths never share data.

### Discovery

Only visible by reading the service implementation source. The class name and method signatures suggest a uniform reactive abstraction. Not mentioned in any documentation.

### Impact on tests

Tests that call `sendMessage()` then verify the timeline via `getChannelTimeline()` always see an empty timeline in test setups using `@Alternative` in-memory stores, even though the message was "successfully" posted.

### Fix / Workaround

Identify the actual data path for each operation you need to test:
1. Check which CDI service field the MCP tool method delegates to
2. Check whether that service uses CDI store injection or Panache statics
3. For end-to-end write→read tests: use the real H2 database (don't rely on in-memory stores for both sides)

Alternatively, file an upstream issue to make the read path use the same abstraction as the write path.

**See also:** [GE-20260512-66d997](GE-20260512-66d997.md) — Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores

entries.title: quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives silently ignores %dev. profile overrides — use exclude-types instead
entries.body:
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives silently ignores %dev. profile overrides — use exclude-types instead

### Symptom
`%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` is set in `application.properties` alongside a non-profiled `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives`. In dev mode, the `@Alternative` beans listed in the `%dev.` value are never activated — ArC throws `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` for the interfaces they implement, even though the beans exist on the classpath and have Jandex indices.

### Context
Multi-profile Quarkus application needing different CDI alternatives per environment:
- Production: reactive JPA stores (`engine-persistence-hibernate`)
- Dev mode: in-memory blocking stores (`engine-persistence-memory`)
- Tests: separate `application.properties` with its own `selected-alternatives`

Both `%dev.` and non-profiled values present in the same `application.properties`.

### What was tried (didn't work)
1. `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=MemoryPlanItemStore,...` alongside non-profiled `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=JpaPlanItemStore,...` — dev alternatives silently ignored
2. Removing the non-profiled value and using only `%dev.` and `%prod.` — still ignored
3. Verified `%dev.quarkus.arc.exclude-types` DOES work (OidcCurrentPrincipal successfully excluded in dev) — confirms profile overrides work for other ArC properties

### Root cause
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` is a build-time fixed property that does not honour `%profile.` prefixes. The property is resolved once during Quarkus augmentation and profile-specific values are silently discarded. This is not documented — the Quarkus configuration reference does not distinguish which ArC properties support profile overrides.

### Fix
Select ALL alternatives (both JPA and memory) in the non-profiled `selected-alternatives`. Use profile-specific `exclude-types` to control which survive in each environment:

```properties
# Non-profiled: select everything — both JPA and memory alternatives
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.persistence.jpa.JpaPlanItemStore,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryPlanItemStore,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository

# Dev: exclude reactive JPA stores → memory stores win
%dev.quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.persistence.jpa.JpaPlanItemStore,...

# Prod: exclude memory stores → JPA stores win
%prod.quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryPlanItemStore,...
```

ArC processes `exclude-types` first (removing beans entirely), then resolves `selected-alternatives` among surviving beans. In each profile, only one alternative per interface survives — no ambiguity.

### Why this is non-obvious
Profile-specific overrides (`%dev.`, `%prod.`) work for most Quarkus properties, including `quarkus.arc.exclude-types`. The natural assumption is that `selected-alternatives` follows the same pattern. There is no error, warning, or log message when the profile override is ignored — the non-profiled value is silently used. The failure manifests as `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` with no hint about profile resolution.

### Why this fix
`exclude-types` is a coarser mechanism (removes the bean entirely vs just not selecting it), but it's the only ArC property that supports profile overrides. The exclude-types-first-then-resolve pattern is architecturally sound: each profile starts from the same selected set and carves out what doesn't belong.

### Alternatives considered
- **Maven profiles** — add/remove JARs per profile. Rejected: adds build complexity and doesn't work with `quarkus:dev` which uses the default Maven profile.
- **`@IfBuildProfile("dev")` wrapper beans** — create dev-only `@ApplicationScoped` beans that delegate to memory stores. Rejected: code duplication across 5+ SPIs, maintenance burden.
- **Separate `application-dev.properties`** — Quarkus doesn't support this pattern (it's a Spring Boot convention).

entries.title: InMemoryWorkItemStore.scan() filters by currentPrincipal.tenancyId() — explicit tenancyId on request makes WorkItems invisible
entries.body:
InMemoryWorkItemStore.scan() filters by currentPrincipal.tenancyId() — explicit tenancyId on request makes WorkItems invisible

### Symptom
WorkItem is created successfully (handler logs confirm creation) but `workItemStore.scanAll()` returns an empty list. No error, no warning — the WorkItem silently vanishes from query results.

### Context
Occurs in `@QuarkusTest` suites using `InMemoryWorkItemStore` (the `@Alternative @Priority(1)` test implementation). The test creates a WorkItem via `WorkItemCreator.create(request)` with `tenancyId` set explicitly on the `WorkItemCreateRequest`, then queries with `workItemStore.scanAll()`.

### What was tried (didn't work)
- Verified the WorkItem was created (handler log confirmed)
- Checked CDI wiring (WorkItemSpiAdapter correctly resolved)
- Verified InMemoryWorkItemStore was the active alternative

### Root cause
`InMemoryWorkItemStore` has an asymmetry between `put()` and `scan()`:

- `put()` stamps `tenancyId` from `currentPrincipal.tenancyId()` **only when the WorkItem's tenancyId is null**
- `scan()` (and therefore `scanAll()`) filters results by `currentPrincipal.tenancyId()` **always**

When the `WorkItemCreateRequest` sets `tenancyId` explicitly (e.g. `"test-tenant"`), the value flows through `WorkItemService.create()` to the entity. `put()` sees a non-null tenancyId and does not override it. But `scan()` filters by whatever `currentPrincipal.tenancyId()` returns — which may be a different value (e.g. the platform default tenant).

Result: the WorkItem is in the store's `ConcurrentHashMap` but filtered out by every query.

### Fix
Do not set `tenancyId` on the `WorkItemCreateRequest` in test contexts. Let `InMemoryWorkItemStore.put()` stamp it from `currentPrincipal`. This ensures `put()` and `scan()` use the same tenant value.

```java
// BAD — tenancyId may not match currentPrincipal
WorkItemCreateRequest.builder()
    .title("Review")
    .tenancyId(event.tenancyId())  // explicit — bypasses put() stamp
    .build();

// GOOD — let the store stamp tenancyId from currentPrincipal
WorkItemCreateRequest.builder()
    .title("Review")
    // no .tenancyId() — put() stamps from currentPrincipal
    .build();
```

### Why this is non-obvious
The old code path (direct entity mutation + `workItem.persist()`) never hit this because `persist()` goes to JPA/Panache, not `InMemoryWorkItemStore.put()`. The tenancy filtering only matters when both creation and querying go through the in-memory store — which happens when migrating from runtime service calls to SPI calls (`WorkItemCreator`).

### Alternatives considered
- Setting `currentPrincipal.tenancyId()` to match the explicit value — possible but requires test infrastructure changes and couples tests to tenant identity
- Removing the tenancy filter from `InMemoryWorkItemStore.scan()` — would break multi-tenant test isolation

entries.title: Panache count(String, Object...) prepends FROM Entity WHERE — passing 'WHERE predicate' causes double-WHERE syntax error
entries.body:
Panache count(String, Object...) prepends FROM Entity WHERE — passing 'WHERE predicate' causes double-WHERE syntax error

## Symptom

Runtime SQL syntax error — e.g. `SELECT count(*) FROM Message WHERE WHERE 1=1 AND channelId = ?1`. Tests using InMemory store alternatives pass; only the JPA integration test catches it.

## Context

Adding a `count(MessageQuery)` method to a Quarkus Panache JPA store. The store already has a `scan()` method that builds full JPQL: `"FROM Message WHERE " + predicateString`. By analogy, the developer passes `"WHERE " + predicateString` to `Entity.count(...)`.

## What was tried

```java
MessageQueryJpql mq = MessageQueryJpql.from(q);
return Message.count("WHERE " + mq.where(), mq.params()); // Wrong
```

## Root cause

Panache's `count(String, Object...)` treats its argument as a WHERE predicate fragment — it internally prepends `FROM Entity WHERE`. Passing `"WHERE 1=1"` produces `FROM Message WHERE WHERE 1=1` — a SQL syntax error.

Panache's `find()` / `list()` take full JPQL (`"FROM Entity WHERE ..."`), which conditions developers to include the WHERE keyword explicitly. `count()` uses a different convention — predicate-only — and this distinction is not prominent in the surface docs.

## Fix

Pass just the predicate fragment — no `WHERE` keyword:

```java
MessageQueryJpql mq = MessageQueryJpql.from(q);
return Message.count(mq.where(), mq.params()); // Correct — "1=1 AND channelId = ?1"
```

## Why this is non-obvious

When a store has both `scan()` (full JPQL) and `count()` alongside each other, the natural assumption is that both take the same format. They don't. `count()` is predicate-only. The discrepancy is invisible until the JPA execution path is exercised — InMemory store tests pass regardless, because they never call Panache.

entries.title: Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently
entries.body:
Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently

## Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently

### Symptom

Test suite activates `@Alternative @Priority(1)` InMemory stores via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives`. Panache static queries (`Entity.<Entity>listAll()`, `Entity.find(...)`) return zero results with no error, even though data was inserted through the store.

### Root cause

Panache static methods resolve directly against JPA at the persistence layer, bypassing CDI entirely. `@Alternative` implementations intercept calls made through injected CDI interfaces, not through static Panache entry points. With InMemory stores active, the JPA entity tables are empty — Panache statics query empty tables and return zero results silently.

### Fix

In test code that activates InMemory stores, never use Panache statics to query domain entities. Always go through the injected store or service layer:

```java
// Wrong — bypasses CDI alternative, queries empty JPA table
List<Channel> channels = Channel.<Channel>listAll();

// Correct — routes through the injected alternative store
List<Channel> channels = channelStore.findAll();
// or via service/tool layer
var result = tools.listChannels();
```

### Why this is non-obvious

Panache statics look like repository methods and feel like they should respect CDI alternatives — but they don't. No error is thrown, no warning is logged; the query simply returns an empty collection. The only signal is silently wrong test data.

**See also:** [GE-20260429-a79d0e](GE-20260429-a79d0e.md) — @Alternative @Priority auto-activates without quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives

**See also:** [GE-20260519-f33c66](GE-20260519-f33c66.md) — 'Reactive' service classes may use Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative doesn't intercept

**See also:** [GE-20260528-3d3847](GE-20260528-3d3847.md) — @Transactional on @BeforeAll static methods has no effect (same CDI proxy limitation)

**See also:** [GE-20260608-d223af](GE-20260608-d223af.md) — REST Assured GPath $.findAll returns null (not []) — Hamcrest not(empty()) silently passes on null

**See also:** [GE-20260609-49bd08](GE-20260609-49bd08.md) — static Panache entity calls bypass injected store tenant filtering

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives

## Symptom

Test assertions calling `workItemStore.scanAll()` return empty even though the handler log confirms `WorkItemService.create()` ran successfully. `planItem.getStatus()` is `RUNNING` (proving the handler completed), but the store shows nothing.

## Context

Two `WorkItemStore` beans on the test classpath:
- `JpaWorkItemStore @ApplicationScoped` — production bean from `casehub-work`
- `InMemoryWorkItemStore @Alternative @Priority(1)` — test replacement from `casehub-work-testing`

CDI 4.x spec says `@Priority` on an alternative activates it globally and it should win over non-alternatives. In Quarkus ARC 3.32.2 this does not hold when the alternative comes from an external dependency JAR.

## What was tried

- Adding `InMemoryWorkItemStore` to `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` alone → "Ambiguous dependencies" deployment failure (both beans active simultaneously)
- `quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.work.runtime.repository.jpa.JpaWorkItemStore` alone → no effect; `JpaWorkItemStore` still wins
- `@Inject InMemoryWorkItemStore workItemStore` (concrete type) in the test → bypasses CDI alternative selection; test clears the right store but `WorkItemService` still writes to JPA (H2), so `scanAll()` returns empty

## Root Cause

`@Alternative @Priority(1)` from a dependency JAR is not treated as overriding a non-alternative `@ApplicationScoped` bean by Quarkus ARC. CDI spec §2.6.2 requires `@Priority` alternatives to take precedence, but ARC's resolution appears to treat both as equally active candidates when neither is the local application class. The non-alternative wins or ambiguity is raised.

## Fix

Combine both in `src/test/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
# Remove the JPA impl from CDI discovery entirely
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.work.runtime.repository.jpa.JpaWorkItemStore

# Explicitly select the in-memory replacement
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.work.testing.InMemoryWorkItemStore,\
  ...other alternatives...
```

With `JpaWorkItemStore` excluded, `InMemoryWorkItemStore` is the only active `WorkItemStore` bean. Explicit `selected-alternatives` listing then activates it. Both `WorkItemService` (via `@Inject WorkItemStore`) and the test (via `@Inject InMemoryWorkItemStore`) now reference the same singleton, and `scanAll()` finds what `create()` stored.

## Why this is non-obvious

CDI 4.x spec (and Quarkus docs on alternatives) state that `@Alternative @Priority(n)` globally activates and prioritises the alternative. A developer familiar with CDI 4.x would expect `@Priority(1)` to simply work. The failure is silent — no warning is emitted, `JpaWorkItemStore` just wins quietly. The symptom (empty `scanAll()`) points toward a different root cause (session caching, test isolation) and sends debugging in the wrong direction.

**See also:** [GE-20260429-a79d0e](GE-20260429-a79d0e.md) — @Alternative @Priority auto-activates within same module | [GE-20260521-4de4f1](GE-20260521-4de4f1.md) — TestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely

**See also:** [GE-20260601-cee623](GE-20260601-cee623.md) — QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely

**See also:** [GE-20260601-848232](GE-20260601-848232.md) — exclude-types silently does nothing for beans from Jandex-indexed JARs

**See also:** [GE-20260604-2f0889](GE-20260604-2f0889.md) — @Alternative @Priority(N) stubs appear in @All List<T> alongside original bean

**See also:** [GE-20260616-d70e7e](GE-20260616-d70e7e.md) — selected-alternatives silently accepts non-@Alternative beans

entries.title: 'Reactive' Quarkus service classes may use blocking services or Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative in-memory stores don't intercept those paths
entries.body:
'Reactive' Quarkus service classes may use blocking services or Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative in-memory stores don't intercept those paths

### What is undocumented

A service class annotated `@Alternative` and named with "Reactive" may internally use a `blockingService` field (injected blocking CDI service) for write operations and `Entity.find()` (a Panache static) for read operations. CDI `@Alternative @Priority(1)` in-memory stores only intercept at CDI injection points — Panache statics go directly to the underlying database regardless of what alternatives are selected.

### Context

In Quarkus Qhorus (casehubio/qhorus), `ReactiveQhorusMcpTools`:
- `sendMessage()` delegates internally to `blockingMessageService` → `MessageService.send()` → `messageStore.put()` (uses the `MessageStore` CDI alternative, i.e. `InMemoryMessageStore` in tests)
- `getChannelTimeline()` delegates to `blockingGetChannelTimeline()` → `Message.<Message>find(...)` (Panache static — queries H2 directly, bypasses `MessageStore` entirely)

Result: messages written by `sendMessage()` land in `InMemoryMessageStore` (in-memory). Messages read by `getChannelTimeline()` come from H2 (empty in tests). The two paths never share data.

### Discovery

Only visible by reading the service implementation source. The class name and method signatures suggest a uniform reactive abstraction. Not mentioned in any documentation.

### Impact on tests

Tests that call `sendMessage()` then verify the timeline via `getChannelTimeline()` always see an empty timeline in test setups using `@Alternative` in-memory stores, even though the message was "successfully" posted.

### Fix / Workaround

Identify the actual data path for each operation you need to test:
1. Check which CDI service field the MCP tool method delegates to
2. Check whether that service uses CDI store injection or Panache statics
3. For end-to-end write→read tests: use the real H2 database (don't rely on in-memory stores for both sides)

Alternatively, file an upstream issue to make the read path use the same abstraction as the write path.

**See also:** [GE-20260512-66d997](GE-20260512-66d997.md) — Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores

entries.title: casehub-qhorus MessageService methods using Panache static calls bypass InMemory store in @QuarkusTest
Hibernate dirty-check flushes PanacheEntity field writes inside Panache.withSession() — even for in-memory test stores
entries.body:
Hibernate dirty-check flushes PanacheEntity field writes inside Panache.withSession() — even for in-memory test stores

### Symptom
A JPA UPDATE fires for a field write on a `PanacheEntity` subclass, even though:
- The CDI injection test confirms the in-memory store (`InMemoryReactiveChannelStore`) is selected, not the JPA store
- The entity was created with `new Channel()` — never explicitly `persist()`ed or loaded in the current session
- `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` is correctly configured

Error:
```
org.hibernate.QueryParameterException: No argument for named parameter ':tenancyId'
```

### Context
`ReactiveMessageService.dispatch()` wraps the entire dispatch in `Panache.withSession("qhorus", () -> ...)`. Inside that session scope, the in-memory store's `updateLastActivity()` does:

```java
channel.lastActivityAt = Instant.now();
```

`Channel` is a Hibernate-enhanced `PanacheEntity`. The field write is intercepted by Hibernate's dirty tracker within the active session scope and generates a JPA UPDATE at session flush.

### Root cause
Hibernate's bytecode enhancement instruments all `PanacheEntity` subclasses with dirty tracking. Any field write on an enhanced entity within an active `Panache.withSession()` scope is detected and queued for flush — regardless of whether the entity was explicitly persisted or loaded in that session. The CDI injection test proves the right bean is injected, but it does not reveal that Hibernate's session-scoped dirty tracker is intercepting field mutations on enhanced entities.

### Fix
Make the in-memory store's `updateLastActivity()` a no-op. Do not modify enhanced entity fields in-place within a `withSession()` scope:

```java
@Override
public Uni<Void> updateLastActivity(UUID channelId, String tenancyId) {
    // No-op — modifying the Hibernate-enhanced entity in-place triggers
    // a dirty-check flush when called from within Panache.withSession().
    return Uni.createFrom().voidItem();
}
```

`lastActivityAt` is set at channel creation time via `put()`; staleness tracking is irrelevant in test contexts.

### Why this is non-obvious
The CDI direct injection test is a convincing false positive: `@Inject ReactiveChannelStore` returns `InMemoryReactiveChannelStore` ✅. This proves the right bean is injected, but it does not prove that field mutations on Hibernate-enhanced entities are safe within the dispatch session scope. The JPA UPDATE is generated by Hibernate's session-level flush mechanism, not by the in-memory store calling `repo.update()` directly.

entries.title: casehub-engine-persistence-memory @Alternative beans need explicit selected-alternatives for SubCaseGroupRepository and PlanItemStore
entries.body:
casehub-engine-persistence-memory @Alternative beans need explicit selected-alternatives for SubCaseGroupRepository and PlanItemStore

### Symptom

Quarkus startup fails with:

```
jakarta.enterprise.inject.spi.DeploymentException: Found 2 deployment problems:
[1] Unsatisfied dependency for type io.casehub.engine.common.spi.SubCaseGroupRepository
    - injection target: SubCaseExecutionHandler constructor
[2] Unsatisfied dependency for type io.casehub.engine.common.spi.SubCaseGroupRepository
    - injection target: SubCaseCompletionService constructor
```

### Context

When a CaseHub harness adds `casehub-engine-blackboard` (required for SubCase lifecycle and M-of-N quorum) alongside `casehub-engine-persistence-memory`, the blackboard beans (`SubCaseExecutionHandler`, `SubCaseCompletionService`) need `SubCaseGroupRepository`. The in-memory implementation (`MemorySubCaseGroupRepository`) exists in persistence-memory but is `@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` — CDI does not activate `@Alternative` beans unless explicitly selected.

The same applies to `MemoryPlanItemStore` and `MemoryReactivePlanItemStore` from the same module.

### What was tried (didn't work)

- Adding Jandex index entries for persistence-memory — beans are discovered but not activated (they're @Alternative)
- Assuming engine-testing would provide @Priority(1) overrides — engine-testing has TestCaseMetaModelRepository, TestCaseInstanceRepository, TestEventLogRepository but NOT TestSubCaseGroupRepository or TestPlanItemStore

### Root cause

`casehub-engine-persistence-memory` ships all repository implementations as `@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` — they require explicit `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` to be activated. Engine-testing provides `@Priority(1)` overrides for CaseInstanceRepository and EventLogRepository but not for SubCaseGroupRepository or PlanItemStore.

### Fix

Add to `application.properties` (test or production):

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemorySubCaseGroupRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryPlanItemStore,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryReactivePlanItemStore
```

Append to existing `selected-alternatives` if other beans (e.g. `JpaLedgerEntryRepository`) are already listed.

### Why this is non-obvious

The error names `SubCaseGroupRepository` — but `MemorySubCaseGroupRepository` IS on the classpath and IS Jandex-indexed. The CDI error doesn't mention that the bean exists but is an unselected `@Alternative`. You have to read the source of `MemorySubCaseGroupRepository` to discover the `@Alternative` annotation, then check whether engine-testing provides an override (it doesn't for these SPIs).

## Variant — Runtime failure from implicit activation of remaining @Alternative beans

### Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` starts cleanly (no DeploymentException) because `casehub-engine-testing` provides `@Priority(1)` overrides for `CaseInstanceRepository` and `EventLogRepository`. But the first `startCase()` call in certain test classes fails with:

```
(RECIPIENT_FAILURE,8191) CaseInstance not found or wrong tenant: <uuid>
```

The error comes from `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository.update()` (persistence-memory) — the bean is implicitly activated by Quarkus Arc as the sole `@Alternative` without a competing non-alternative, but the activation path produces intermittent initialization-order failures.

### Context

When only `SubCaseGroupRepository` and `PlanItemStore` are in `selected-alternatives`, the remaining 4 `@Alternative` beans from persistence-memory rely on Arc's implicit fallback activation:
- `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository`
- `InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository`
- `InMemoryEventLogRepository`
- `MemoryReactivePlanItemStore`

This implicit activation works for most test classes but fails intermittently when the test class is among the first to trigger `startCase()` in a fresh JVM — the `CaseStartedEventHandler` calls `caseInstanceRepository.update()` and the instance isn't found.

### Fix

Add all 4 remaining persistence-memory `@Alternative` beans to `selected-alternatives`:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  ...,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryEventLogRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryReactivePlanItemStore
```

Verified in casehub-aml#58: 4 of 6 failing test classes fixed by explicit selection. 2 remaining failures (`AmlLayer7ResourceTest`, `AmlTrustRoutingAttestationTest`) may have a deeper root cause — investigation ongoing.

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index

## Context

Building a `casehub-testing` module to provide `@Alternative @Priority(1)` in-memory repository implementations that downstream `@QuarkusTest` classes can consume by just adding the module as a test dependency — no `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` config needed.

## Symptom

```
jakarta.enterprise.inject.UnsatisfiedResolutionException: Unsatisfied dependency
  for type io.casehub.engine.spi.CaseInstanceRepository and qualifiers [@Default]
```

Downstream `@QuarkusTest` fails to start even though `casehub-testing` is on the classpath and its `TestCaseInstanceRepository` carries `@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped`.

## Root Cause

Quarkus CDI bean discovery requires a Jandex index to scan beans from classpath JARs. If the testing helper JAR does not build a Jandex index (i.e., the `jandex-maven-plugin` or equivalent is not configured), Quarkus cannot see any of its beans — including `@Alternative @Priority(1)` classes. The annotation is present in the bytecode but the JAR is effectively invisible to CDI.

The `casehub-testing` pom.xml had no Jandex configuration, so its beans were never indexed.

## Fix

**Option A (recommended for reusable testing jars):** Add Jandex indexing to the helper module's pom.xml:

```xml
<plugin>
    <groupId>io.smallrye</groupId>
    <artifactId>jandex-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    <executions>
        <execution>
            <id>make-index</id>
            <goals><goal>jandex</goal></goals>
        </execution>
    </executions>
</plugin>
```

**Option B (proven fallback):** Keep in-memory implementations in `casehub-persistence-memory` without `@Priority`, and activate them via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `src/test/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryEventLogRepository
```

Option B is what every other module in the project already uses and is the safe, proven path.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The `@Alternative @Priority(1)` pattern is supposed to be self-activating — you expect it to "just work" when the JAR is on the classpath. The failure message says "unsatisfied dependency" which points to a missing implementation, not to a missing index. Nothing in the error or Quarkus startup log mentions Jandex.

**See also:** [GE-20260423-a01832](GE-20260423-a01832.md) — @Alternative beans in third-party test jars not scanned without explicit index config | [GE-20260421-03fa72](GE-20260421-03fa72.md) — optional module jar missing Jandex index | [GE-20260417-c59817](GE-20260417-c59817.md) — quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives activates @Alternative beans | [GE-20260427-62d3ab](GE-20260427-62d3ab.md) — @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans as test spies | [GE-20260424-59906a](GE-20260424-59906a.md) — CDI does not scan @ApplicationScoped beans in plain JAR module | [GE-0133](GE-0133.md) — CDI silently ignores beans in jars without Jandex index

**See also:** GE-20260422-ebb91d — CDI AmbiguousResolutionException when multiple @ApplicationScoped beans implement the same SPI interface
**See also:** [GE-20260415-884e48 — @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException](GE-20260415-884e48.md)
