entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

## @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

### Stack
Quarkus 3.31.1, CDI 4.0

### Symptom
Multiple beans implement the same SPI interface. Each alternative is annotated with
`@Alternative @Priority(10)` as well as `@ApplicationScoped`. On Quarkus startup, the
application fails with `AmbiguousResolutionException` — all implementations are active
simultaneously.

```
AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type ContextDiffStrategy
and qualifiers [@Default]: [TopLevelContextDiffStrategy, JsonPatchContextDiffStrategy, 
NoOpContextDiffStrategy]
```

### Context
SPI pattern where one default bean and two `@Alternative` beans implement an interface.
The intent was to activate one alternative at a time via
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`.

### What was tried
Adding `@Priority(10)` alongside `@Alternative` — expected this to be a ranking hint
that would help CDI resolve ambiguity. Instead, `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is what
globally activates the bean in CDI 4.0, making all three implementations simultaneously
active and causing the ambiguous resolution error.

### Root cause
In CDI 4.0 (Jakarta EE 10), `@Alternative @Priority(n)` is a shorthand for globally
activating an alternative across the entire application — no `beans.xml` or
`selected-alternatives` config needed. When ALL alternatives carry this annotation,
they are ALL active, which breaks injection points expecting a single bean.

### Solution 1 — Remove @Priority, use selected-alternatives config
**Approach:** Strip `@Priority` from all alternatives; activate one via config.
**Pros:** Per-profile/per-module control without code changes; the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching.
**Cons:** Requires a config entry per activation; all alternatives default to inactive.

Remove `@Priority` from all `@Alternative` beans. Activate a specific alternative only
via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`:

```java
// WRONG — @Priority globally activates the alternative in CDI 4.0
@Alternative
@Priority(10)
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }

// CORRECT — @Alternative only; activation controlled by config
@Alternative
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }
```

```properties
# application.properties — activate the desired alternative
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.MyAlternativeImpl
```

### Solution 2 — Use different @Priority values to establish precedence
**Approach:** Keep `@Alternative @Priority` but assign distinct priority values; CDI resolves to the highest.
**Pros:** No config entry needed; the default (lowest priority) is always active, the override (highest priority) wins when present on the classpath. Works naturally for optional-module patterns where a higher-capability implementation should auto-replace the baseline.
**Cons:** All alternatives are always globally active (the core CDI 4.0 behaviour). The "default" must have the lowest priority; adding a third implementation requires careful priority assignment.

```java
// Default — always active; lowest priority loses when a higher one is present
@Alternative
@Priority(0)
@ApplicationScoped
public class ClaimFirstStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }

// Override — higher priority wins; active only when on the classpath
@Alternative
@Priority(1)
@ApplicationScoped
public class SemanticWorkerSelectionStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }
```

CDI injects the `@Priority(1)` bean when both are present; falls back to `@Priority(0)` when only
the default module is on the classpath. No `beans.xml` or `selected-alternatives` required.

**When to choose Solution 2 over Solution 1:** When the override is an optional module (not always
on the classpath) and the default must always be present. Config-based activation (Solution 1)
requires an explicit entry even for the default case; priority-based resolution (Solution 2) makes
the default zero-configuration.

### Why this is non-obvious
The `@Priority` annotation is commonly used alongside `@Alternative` in CDI tutorials
and examples from the CDI 2.x era, where `@Priority` on an alternative was a *ranking*
hint. In CDI 4.0 the semantics changed — `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is now an
*activation* mechanism (equivalent to listing it in `beans.xml` with a priority). The
Quarkus docs for `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` don't warn about this interaction.

### Why this fix
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` gives per-profile/per-module control via config,
which is the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching (same as how `PanningStrategyLoopControl`
and other engine SPIs are activated). It doesn't require code changes to switch backends.

**See also:** GE-20260422-ebb91d — AmbiguousResolutionException from multiple unqualified @ApplicationScoped beans

**See also:** GE-20260427-97650e "CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface"
**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab — use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f — @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md)

### Update — Test-refactoring trap: two classes declaring the same @Alternative

During a test refactoring, if two `@QuarkusTest` classes *simultaneously* declare an
`@Alternative @Priority(2)` inner class implementing the same type, Quarkus boot fails
with `AmbiguousResolutionException` even though `mvn test-compile` passes cleanly. The
compile succeeds because both classes are syntactically valid; the CDI conflict only
surfaces at application startup.

entries.title: SPLADE (MS MARCO) has zero Java domain vocabulary — expands ChatModel to hotel/beauty/renovation
entries.body:
SPLADE (MS MARCO) has zero Java domain vocabulary — expands ChatModel to hotel/beauty/renovation

### Symptom
Adding SPLADE sparse embeddings to dense retrieval (RRF fusion) churns result sets without improving quality for Java/CDI domain queries. All 28 queries changed, 3 highly relevant entries were displaced while 40 noise entries were removed — net effect ambiguous.

### Context
Hortora engine hybrid search benchmark (#28). SPLADE (`Splade_PP_en_v1`) added as sparse embedding alongside nomic-embed-text dense vectors. RRF fusion (k=60, denseTopK=40, sparseTopK=40). Tested across 14 real-world scenarios covering Java class names, CDI annotations, JPA patterns, and Quarkus framework terms.

SPLADE vocabulary analysis decoded the activated tokens for each query:

| Query | Top SPLADE Expansions | Expected (Java domain) |
|-------|----------------------|----------------------|
| `DefaultBean\|AmbiguousResolutionException` | ambiguity, groups, unclear | cdi, qualifier, alternative, inject |
| `ChatModel\|AgentSession\|LangChain4j` | agent, talk, beauty, renovation, genre | model, inference, provider, adapter |
| `Priority(100)\|CDI priority` | priorities, cds (music), urgency, precedence | qualifier, scope, alternative, bean |

Zero Tier 1 domain term activations across all 14 KW queries. Not one activation of `panache`, `jandex`, `qualifier`, `interceptor`, `singleton`, `persist`, or any unambiguous Java/CDI term.

### Root cause
Two compounding failures:

1. **BERT WordPiece tokenizer** fragments Java class names into meaningless subwords: `AmbiguousResolutionException` → `["am", "##bi", "##guous", "##reso", "##lution", "##exception"]`. The semantic content is destroyed before SPLADE runs.

2. **MS MARCO training data** contains no Java ecosystem content. SPLADE's learned vocabulary expansion maps "chat" to web-domain associations (hotels, beauty, renovation) — not to software engineering concepts.

This is expected behaviour for this model architecture, not a defect. The same limitation applies to nomic-embed-text (see GE-20260627-4712de) — the difference is that SPLADE actively ADDS noise through irrelevant expansions, whereas nomic-embed-text merely fails to discriminate.

### Fix
Do not use general-purpose (MS MARCO-trained) SPLADE models for Java/CDI domain retrieval. Three alternatives:

1. **BM25 keyword matching** (casehubio/neural-text#47/#48) — exact token matching via Qdrant full-text index. Directly addresses the vocabulary gap.
2. **Code-domain SPLADE model** (casehubio/neural-text#49) — if one exists with Java training data.
3. **Code-domain dense embedding model** (casehubio/neural-text#49) — models like CodeBERT, UniXcoder trained on source code.

### Why this is non-obvious
SPLADE is presented as a vocabulary expansion technique that recovers keyword precision lost by dense embeddings. The expectation is that it bridges the gap between semantic search and keyword matching. For general-domain web queries, this is true. For Java class names, it actively makes things worse by introducing irrelevant web-domain associations into the sparse vector.

### Why this fix
BM25 is the highest-leverage fix because it directly solves the failure mode (exact token matching for Java identifiers) without requiring model training or fine-tuning. Code-domain models are the long-term architectural fix but have higher uncertainty and longer timeline.

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(N) in Quarkus CDI auto-activates without quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives config
CDI StartupEvent @Priority ordering: no-priority observer fires at 2500, after @Priority(10) — not before
entries.body:
CDI StartupEvent @Priority ordering: no-priority observer fires at 2500, after @Priority(10) — not before

## Symptom

A `@Observes StartupEvent` method with no `@Priority` annotation appears to run *before* another observer annotated with `@Priority(10)`. In reality, the opposite is true: the undecorated observer fires at the CDI default priority (2500), which is *much later* than `@Priority(10)`.

The bug manifests as a startup workaround that "seems to work" because the system falls back to a sensible default — masking the fact that the CDI-injected value never reached its target in time.

**Real case:** `ObjectMapperInjector @Observes StartupEvent` (no @Priority) was meant to inject a CDI-managed `ObjectMapper` into a static field on `CaseDefinitionYamlMapper` before case definitions loaded. `DefaultCaseDefinitionRegistry.onStart(@Priority(10))` loaded definitions eagerly. The injector fired at priority 2500 — *after* the registry — so the CDI ObjectMapper never participated in definition loading. The fallback `new ObjectMapper(new YAMLFactory())` worked well enough that the bug went unnoticed for months.

## Root cause

CDI event observer priority follows the same semantics as Java interceptor priority: **lower number = higher priority = fires first**. CDI 4.0 specifies the default observer priority as `javax.interceptor.Interceptor.Priority.APPLICATION + 500 = 2500`.

- `@Priority(10)` → fires very early (almost first)
- No `@Priority` → fires at 2500 (application-tier default)

A developer expecting "no annotation = fires in some undefined order near the observer" will be surprised that it always fires *after* any explicitly-prioritised observer.

## Fix

Either:

1. Give the startup injector an explicit `@Priority` lower than any observer it must run before:
   ```java
   void onStartup(@Observes @Priority(1) StartupEvent event) { ... }
   ```

2. Eliminate the static injection pattern entirely. If the observer's target is a CDI bean, inject the dependency directly rather than via a static setter:
   ```java
   @Inject @YamlMapper ObjectMapper objectMapper; // injected before any StartupEvent fires
   ```
   CDI field injection completes *before* any `StartupEvent` observers fire, regardless of priority.

## Why this is non-obvious

The natural reading of "no @Priority = unordered" aligns with how many other frameworks handle default ordering. CDI's explicit default of 2500 is buried in the spec and not surfaced by Quarkus tooling. A static setter that "works" despite wrong ordering hides the bug indefinitely — the fallback default happens to be functionally acceptable.

### Why this fix

Option 2 (direct CDI injection) is the correct design because it removes the static mutable state entirely. Option 1 merely papers over the ordering problem without fixing the architectural smell.

**See also:** [GE-20260522-5685ba](GE-20260522-5685ba.md) — synchronous CDI Event.fire() in @Observes StartupEvent propagates observer exceptions

entries.title: @IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs
entries.body:
@IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs

### Symptom

Adding a dev-profile-only CDI bean (e.g., a `CurrentPrincipal` for dev mode) via `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` causes startup failures — all beans from the non-profiled `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` list disappear.

### Context

Quarkus profile-specific properties (`%dev.`, `%test.`, `%prod.`) completely **replace** their non-profiled counterparts. If the base `application.properties` has:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  com.example.JpaRepo,\
  com.example.JpaPlanItemStore,\
  com.example.JpaSubCaseGroupRepo
```

Adding `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` drops all three JPA alternatives in dev mode — CDI startup fails with unsatisfied dependency errors for every bean that was in the original list.

### Root cause

Profile-specific properties in Quarkus are full replacements, not merges. This is by design (SmallRye Config behaviour), but the failure mode is silent — no warning that the non-profiled value was dropped.

### Fix

Use bean-level annotations instead of `selected-alternatives`:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
@Priority(150)  // beats OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)
@IfBuildProfile("dev")
public class DemoCurrentPrincipal implements CurrentPrincipal {
    @Override public String tenancyId() { return "demo-tenant"; }
    @Override public String actorId()   { return "demo-user"; }
}
```

**Requirements:**
1. The bean must live in a **Jandex-indexed JAR** (e.g., the application's own `runtime/` module). ArC discovers it automatically — no `selected-alternatives` entry needed.
2. `@IfBuildProfile("dev")` excludes the bean entirely from non-dev builds — no ambiguity in production.
3. `@Priority(150)` resolves over competing `@Alternative` beans (e.g., `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)`) via standard ArC priority resolution.

This is the same mechanism by which `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Alternative @Priority(100)` beats `MockCurrentPrincipal @DefaultBean` in production — without being listed in `selected-alternatives`.

### Why this is non-obvious

The natural instinct is `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=MyDevBean` — it looks like the standard profile-override pattern. The replacement behaviour is documented for SmallRye Config but not prominently flagged in the Quarkus CDI guide. The failure (dropped JPA alternatives) manifests as unrelated `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` errors that don't mention `selected-alternatives` at all.

### Why this fix

`@IfBuildProfile + @Priority` solves the problem at the bean level — no configuration property needed. The alternative (duplicating the full `selected-alternatives` list in every profile override) is fragile and drifts as new alternatives are added.

### Alternatives considered

- **Duplicate the full list in `%dev.`** — works but fragile; every new `@Alternative` bean must be added to every profile-specific override. Drifts silently.
- **Use `@UnlessBuildProfile("prod")` instead of `@IfBuildProfile("dev")`** — activates in both dev and test profiles, which may not be desired. Test profile has its own `FixedCurrentPrincipal @Priority(200)`.

entries.title: Removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation CDI chain silently changes resolution — no AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
Removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation CDI chain silently changes resolution — no AmbiguousResolutionException

### Symptom

After removing `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from one CDI bean, a different bean
silently becomes the active implementation. No `AmbiguousResolutionException`, no
warning, no log. Tests that depend on the removed bean's behaviour fail with
unrelated-looking assertion errors.

### Context

Three implementations of the same port interface coexist in the build:

| Bean | Annotations | Role |
|------|------------|------|
| `PrReviewService` | `@ApplicationScoped @DefaultBean` | Layer 1 naive baseline |
| `QhorusPrReviewService` | `@ApplicationScoped @Alternative @Priority(1)` | Layer 3 qhorus messaging |
| `PrReviewCaseService` | `@ApplicationScoped @Alternative @Priority(2)` | Layer 5 engine (active) |

This is the CDI displacement pattern for progressive layer integration — each layer
adds an implementation; CDI priority determines which is active at runtime.

### What was tried (didn't work)

Removed `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from `PrReviewCaseService` to fix an anti-pattern
documented in ARC42STORIES §8 ("CDI priority gymnastics"). Expected: the plain
`@ApplicationScoped` bean would still displace the `@DefaultBean` baseline via
Quarkus's `@DefaultBean` semantics.

### Root cause

CDI resolution rules interact in a non-obvious way:

1. `@DefaultBean` beans are suppressed when any non-`@DefaultBean` bean exists ✅
2. Activated `@Alternative` beans (with `@Priority`) take precedence over regular beans ⚠️
3. After removing `@Alternative` from `PrReviewCaseService`, it becomes a regular bean
4. `QhorusPrReviewService` (`@Alternative @Priority(1)`) is still an activated alternative
5. **Rule 2 applies**: the activated alternative wins over the regular bean
6. `QhorusPrReviewService` becomes the active implementation — silently

The mental model "I'm just removing unnecessary annotations" is wrong. The annotations
are load-bearing: they participate in a priority chain where every bean's annotation
set matters.

### Fix

All implementations in the `@Alternative` chain must be addressed together, not one at
a time. To properly fix the anti-pattern:

- Either make ALL non-baseline implementations use `@DefaultBean` displacement
  (remove `@Alternative @Priority` from all of them and ensure only one non-`@DefaultBean` exists)
- Or keep the `@Alternative @Priority` chain intact and accept it as the mechanism
  for layer coexistence

Do not remove `@Alternative` from one bean without checking what other `@Alternative`
implementations exist for the same interface.

### Why this is non-obvious

Most developers reason about CDI alternatives in pairs: "my bean vs the default."
The three-way interaction (DefaultBean + Alternative(1) + Alternative(2)) is rarely
encountered, and the silent resolution change (no exception, wrong bean) gives no
diagnostic signal. The symptom (test assertion failure on unrelated data) is
completely disconnected from the cause (CDI annotation change).

### Why this fix

The obvious approach — "just remove the anti-pattern annotation" — is wrong because
it treats the annotations as decoration rather than as a load-bearing priority chain.
The fix requires understanding the full set of implementations and their annotation
interactions before changing any single one.

### Alternatives considered

- Remove `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from only the active bean → silently breaks resolution (this gotcha)
- Remove `@Alternative` from ALL implementations and use `@DefaultBean` displacement → requires ensuring exactly one non-`@DefaultBean` impl, and retiring or `@DefaultBean`-ing the intermediate layers
- Keep `@Alternative @Priority` chain as-is → accepted as the working mechanism until a coordinated cleanup addresses all implementations together

**See also:** GE-20260626-c21b02 — @DefaultBean suppression by any Instance<T> peer, a different silent failure in the same CDI displacement space.

entries.title: quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives default priority 1 creates tie with @Priority(1) subclass beans — two @ApplicationScoped instances with separate state
entries.body:
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives default priority 1 creates tie with @Priority(1) subclass beans — two @ApplicationScoped instances with separate state

### Symptom
`CaseInstance not found or wrong tenant` (or similar state-not-found error) despite the instance being saved moments earlier. The save and lookup use the same `CaseInstanceRepository` interface, but they resolve to different `@ApplicationScoped` bean instances with separate `ConcurrentHashMap` stores.

### Context
`casehub-engine-testing` provides `TestCaseInstanceRepository extends InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` with `@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped`. This is the standard test harness — it auto-activates without `selected-alternatives`.

A consumer project adds `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` to `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in test `application.properties`, thinking it's redundant but harmless.

### What was tried (didn't work)
- Checking event bus threading and Vert.x serialization — object identity was intact
- Verifying `currentPrincipal.tenancyId()` consistency — MockCurrentPrincipal returns a constant
- Adding more beans to `selected-alternatives` — made it worse

### Root cause
Quarkus Arc assigns a **default priority of 1** to alternatives listed in `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` that don't have `@Priority`. This creates a priority tie:

- `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` — priority 1 (from `selected-alternatives` default)
- `TestCaseInstanceRepository` — priority 1 (from `@Priority(1)`)

Both are `@ApplicationScoped`, so CDI creates **two separate bean instances**. Each has its own `ConcurrentHashMap<UUID, CaseInstance> store`. If CDI resolves different injection points to different beans (or resolves non-deterministically), saves go to one store and lookups to another.

### Fix
Do not add parent classes to `selected-alternatives` when the engine-testing module already provides `@Priority(1)` subclasses:

```properties
# WRONG — creates priority tie with TestCaseInstanceRepository
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=...,io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository

# RIGHT — let engine-testing's @Priority(1) beans handle it
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=...
# (omit InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository, InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository, InMemoryEventLogRepository)
```

The engine-testing module exists specifically to provide auto-activating alternatives for test contexts. Adding the parent classes to `selected-alternatives` defeats the purpose.

### Why this is non-obvious
Adding a class to `selected-alternatives` feels like an explicit, high-confidence activation — surely it would "win" over a `@Priority(1)` subclass. But the default priority is also 1, creating an exact tie. The Quarkus docs mention the default priority but don't warn about ties with existing `@Priority(1)` alternatives on the classpath. The symptom (state not found) appears as a threading or serialization bug, not a CDI resolution issue.

entries.title: @DefaultBean BlockingToReactiveBridge — wrap any blocking SPI as reactive, displaced by native async @Alternative
entries.body:
@DefaultBean BlockingToReactiveBridge — wrap any blocking SPI as reactive, displaced by native async @Alternative

## Context

You have a blocking SPI (`CaseMemoryStore`) in a zero-dep API module and need to expose a
reactive mirror (`ReactiveCaseMemoryStore`) so callers using Mutiny can also use the same
backends. Most blocking adapters don't need true async execution — wrapping them as
`Uni.createFrom().item(supplier)` is sufficient. But occasional adapters (e.g. a graph
database with native async traversal) do benefit from a true reactive implementation.

## What developers reach for instead

- Two independent SPIs with no connection — adapters implement both, duplicating logic
- Make the reactive SPI extend the blocking SPI — creates tight coupling and forces adapters
  to implement both interface families
- A single abstract class with default delegation — doesn't play well with CDI proxies

## The Technique

Define the blocking SPI in the zero-dep API module. Define the reactive SPI separately
(in the Quarkus module, because `Uni<>` depends on Smallrye Mutiny). Ship a
`@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` bridge class that injects the active blocking impl and
wraps each method:

```java
@DefaultBean
@ApplicationScoped
public class BlockingToReactiveBridge implements ReactiveCaseMemoryStore {

@Inject CaseMemoryStore delegate;   // whatever blocking impl is active

@Override
    public Uni<String> store(MemoryInput input) {
        return Uni.createFrom().item(() -> delegate.store(input));
    }

@Override
    public Uni<List<Memory>> query(MemoryQuery query) {
        return Uni.createFrom().item(() -> delegate.query(query));
    }

@Override
    public Uni<Void> erase(EraseRequest request) {
        return Uni.createFrom().voidItem().invoke(() -> delegate.erase(request));
    }
}
```

The CDI priority ladder then becomes:

| Tier | Class | Annotation | Active when |
|------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| 0 | `NoOpCaseMemoryStore` | `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` | No adapter |
| — | `BlockingToReactiveBridge` | `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` | Always (wraps Tier 0–2) |
| 1 | Memori adapter | `@ApplicationScoped` | Memori dep declared |
| 2 | Mem0 adapter | `@Alternative @Priority(1)` | Mem0 dep declared |
| 3 | Graphiti native-reactive | `@Alternative @Priority(2)` on `ReactiveCaseMemoryStore` | Graphiti dep declared |

When Graphiti (Tier 3) is added, it overrides the `ReactiveCaseMemoryStore` binding directly
via its higher-priority `@Alternative` — the bridge's `@DefaultBean` yields automatically.
Tier 3 does NOT need to implement the blocking SPI at all.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The `@DefaultBean` on the bridge is the key: it means both the bridge AND any blocking
adapter can coexist on the classpath without CDI ambiguity. The bridge always yields to a
native-reactive `@Alternative` on `ReactiveCaseMemoryStore`, and the blocking adapter
displaces the no-op on `CaseMemoryStore`. Each priority rung resolves independently.

Without `@DefaultBean`, a plain `@ApplicationScoped` bridge would collide with any real
`ReactiveCaseMemoryStore` implementation (ARC sees two beans for the same type and fails at
startup).

**Note:** Do NOT add `@Blocking` to bridge methods. `ExecutionModelAnnotationsProcessor`
rejects it on plain CDI bean methods returning `Uni` — only framework entrypoints accept it.
Thread dispatch is the entrypoint's responsibility. (See GE-20260528-74914d.)

## See Also

- `GE-20260521-d72294` — blocking and reactive store interfaces with irreconcilable signatures; this bridge avoids the problem by not having one class implement both
- `GE-20260528-74914d` — `@Blocking` rejected on CDI bean methods returning `Uni`

**See also:** [GE-20260620-9d043b](GE-20260620-9d043b.md) — CDI @Decorator double-application through blocking-to-reactive bridge — idempotency guard required (the gotcha this pattern creates)

results.query_text: CDI priority ladder with multiple tiers of Alternative Priority annotations for persistence backend selection where ephemeral in-memory store must override all other backends

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

## @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

### Stack
Quarkus 3.31.1, CDI 4.0

### Symptom
Multiple beans implement the same SPI interface. Each alternative is annotated with
`@Alternative @Priority(10)` as well as `@ApplicationScoped`. On Quarkus startup, the
application fails with `AmbiguousResolutionException` — all implementations are active
simultaneously.

```
AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type ContextDiffStrategy
and qualifiers [@Default]: [TopLevelContextDiffStrategy, JsonPatchContextDiffStrategy, 
NoOpContextDiffStrategy]
```

### Context
SPI pattern where one default bean and two `@Alternative` beans implement an interface.
The intent was to activate one alternative at a time via
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`.

### What was tried
Adding `@Priority(10)` alongside `@Alternative` — expected this to be a ranking hint
that would help CDI resolve ambiguity. Instead, `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is what
globally activates the bean in CDI 4.0, making all three implementations simultaneously
active and causing the ambiguous resolution error.

### Root cause
In CDI 4.0 (Jakarta EE 10), `@Alternative @Priority(n)` is a shorthand for globally
activating an alternative across the entire application — no `beans.xml` or
`selected-alternatives` config needed. When ALL alternatives carry this annotation,
they are ALL active, which breaks injection points expecting a single bean.

### Solution 1 — Remove @Priority, use selected-alternatives config
**Approach:** Strip `@Priority` from all alternatives; activate one via config.
**Pros:** Per-profile/per-module control without code changes; the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching.
**Cons:** Requires a config entry per activation; all alternatives default to inactive.

Remove `@Priority` from all `@Alternative` beans. Activate a specific alternative only
via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`:

```java
// WRONG — @Priority globally activates the alternative in CDI 4.0
@Alternative
@Priority(10)
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }

// CORRECT — @Alternative only; activation controlled by config
@Alternative
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }
```

```properties
# application.properties — activate the desired alternative
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.MyAlternativeImpl
```

### Solution 2 — Use different @Priority values to establish precedence
**Approach:** Keep `@Alternative @Priority` but assign distinct priority values; CDI resolves to the highest.
**Pros:** No config entry needed; the default (lowest priority) is always active, the override (highest priority) wins when present on the classpath. Works naturally for optional-module patterns where a higher-capability implementation should auto-replace the baseline.
**Cons:** All alternatives are always globally active (the core CDI 4.0 behaviour). The "default" must have the lowest priority; adding a third implementation requires careful priority assignment.

```java
// Default — always active; lowest priority loses when a higher one is present
@Alternative
@Priority(0)
@ApplicationScoped
public class ClaimFirstStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }

// Override — higher priority wins; active only when on the classpath
@Alternative
@Priority(1)
@ApplicationScoped
public class SemanticWorkerSelectionStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }
```

CDI injects the `@Priority(1)` bean when both are present; falls back to `@Priority(0)` when only
the default module is on the classpath. No `beans.xml` or `selected-alternatives` required.

**When to choose Solution 2 over Solution 1:** When the override is an optional module (not always
on the classpath) and the default must always be present. Config-based activation (Solution 1)
requires an explicit entry even for the default case; priority-based resolution (Solution 2) makes
the default zero-configuration.

### Why this is non-obvious
The `@Priority` annotation is commonly used alongside `@Alternative` in CDI tutorials
and examples from the CDI 2.x era, where `@Priority` on an alternative was a *ranking*
hint. In CDI 4.0 the semantics changed — `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is now an
*activation* mechanism (equivalent to listing it in `beans.xml` with a priority). The
Quarkus docs for `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` don't warn about this interaction.

### Why this fix
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` gives per-profile/per-module control via config,
which is the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching (same as how `PanningStrategyLoopControl`
and other engine SPIs are activated). It doesn't require code changes to switch backends.

**See also:** GE-20260422-ebb91d — AmbiguousResolutionException from multiple unqualified @ApplicationScoped beans

**See also:** GE-20260427-97650e "CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface"
**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab — use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f — @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md)

### Update — Test-refactoring trap: two classes declaring the same @Alternative

During a test refactoring, if two `@QuarkusTest` classes *simultaneously* declare an
`@Alternative @Priority(2)` inner class implementing the same type, Quarkus boot fails
with `AmbiguousResolutionException` even though `mvn test-compile` passes cleanly. The
compile succeeds because both classes are syntactically valid; the CDI conflict only
surfaces at application startup.

entries.title: casehub-engine-persistence-memory @Alternative beans need explicit selected-alternatives for SubCaseGroupRepository and PlanItemStore
entries.body:
casehub-engine-persistence-memory @Alternative beans need explicit selected-alternatives for SubCaseGroupRepository and PlanItemStore

### Symptom

Quarkus startup fails with:

```
jakarta.enterprise.inject.spi.DeploymentException: Found 2 deployment problems:
[1] Unsatisfied dependency for type io.casehub.engine.common.spi.SubCaseGroupRepository
    - injection target: SubCaseExecutionHandler constructor
[2] Unsatisfied dependency for type io.casehub.engine.common.spi.SubCaseGroupRepository
    - injection target: SubCaseCompletionService constructor
```

### Context

When a CaseHub harness adds `casehub-engine-blackboard` (required for SubCase lifecycle and M-of-N quorum) alongside `casehub-engine-persistence-memory`, the blackboard beans (`SubCaseExecutionHandler`, `SubCaseCompletionService`) need `SubCaseGroupRepository`. The in-memory implementation (`MemorySubCaseGroupRepository`) exists in persistence-memory but is `@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` — CDI does not activate `@Alternative` beans unless explicitly selected.

The same applies to `MemoryPlanItemStore` and `MemoryReactivePlanItemStore` from the same module.

### What was tried (didn't work)

- Adding Jandex index entries for persistence-memory — beans are discovered but not activated (they're @Alternative)
- Assuming engine-testing would provide @Priority(1) overrides — engine-testing has TestCaseMetaModelRepository, TestCaseInstanceRepository, TestEventLogRepository but NOT TestSubCaseGroupRepository or TestPlanItemStore

### Root cause

`casehub-engine-persistence-memory` ships all repository implementations as `@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` — they require explicit `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` to be activated. Engine-testing provides `@Priority(1)` overrides for CaseInstanceRepository and EventLogRepository but not for SubCaseGroupRepository or PlanItemStore.

### Fix

Add to `application.properties` (test or production):

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemorySubCaseGroupRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryPlanItemStore,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryReactivePlanItemStore
```

Append to existing `selected-alternatives` if other beans (e.g. `JpaLedgerEntryRepository`) are already listed.

### Why this is non-obvious

The error names `SubCaseGroupRepository` — but `MemorySubCaseGroupRepository` IS on the classpath and IS Jandex-indexed. The CDI error doesn't mention that the bean exists but is an unselected `@Alternative`. You have to read the source of `MemorySubCaseGroupRepository` to discover the `@Alternative` annotation, then check whether engine-testing provides an override (it doesn't for these SPIs).

## Variant — Runtime failure from implicit activation of remaining @Alternative beans

### Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` starts cleanly (no DeploymentException) because `casehub-engine-testing` provides `@Priority(1)` overrides for `CaseInstanceRepository` and `EventLogRepository`. But the first `startCase()` call in certain test classes fails with:

```
(RECIPIENT_FAILURE,8191) CaseInstance not found or wrong tenant: <uuid>
```

The error comes from `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository.update()` (persistence-memory) — the bean is implicitly activated by Quarkus Arc as the sole `@Alternative` without a competing non-alternative, but the activation path produces intermittent initialization-order failures.

### Context

When only `SubCaseGroupRepository` and `PlanItemStore` are in `selected-alternatives`, the remaining 4 `@Alternative` beans from persistence-memory rely on Arc's implicit fallback activation:
- `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository`
- `InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository`
- `InMemoryEventLogRepository`
- `MemoryReactivePlanItemStore`

This implicit activation works for most test classes but fails intermittently when the test class is among the first to trigger `startCase()` in a fresh JVM — the `CaseStartedEventHandler` calls `caseInstanceRepository.update()` and the instance isn't found.

### Fix

Add all 4 remaining persistence-memory `@Alternative` beans to `selected-alternatives`:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  ...,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryEventLogRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryReactivePlanItemStore
```

Verified in casehub-aml#58: 4 of 6 failing test classes fixed by explicit selection. 2 remaining failures (`AmlLayer7ResourceTest`, `AmlTrustRoutingAttestationTest`) may have a deeper root cause — investigation ongoing.

entries.title: quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives default priority 1 creates tie with @Priority(1) subclass beans — two @ApplicationScoped instances with separate state
entries.body:
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives default priority 1 creates tie with @Priority(1) subclass beans — two @ApplicationScoped instances with separate state

### Symptom
`CaseInstance not found or wrong tenant` (or similar state-not-found error) despite the instance being saved moments earlier. The save and lookup use the same `CaseInstanceRepository` interface, but they resolve to different `@ApplicationScoped` bean instances with separate `ConcurrentHashMap` stores.

### Context
`casehub-engine-testing` provides `TestCaseInstanceRepository extends InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` with `@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped`. This is the standard test harness — it auto-activates without `selected-alternatives`.

A consumer project adds `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` to `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in test `application.properties`, thinking it's redundant but harmless.

### What was tried (didn't work)
- Checking event bus threading and Vert.x serialization — object identity was intact
- Verifying `currentPrincipal.tenancyId()` consistency — MockCurrentPrincipal returns a constant
- Adding more beans to `selected-alternatives` — made it worse

### Root cause
Quarkus Arc assigns a **default priority of 1** to alternatives listed in `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` that don't have `@Priority`. This creates a priority tie:

- `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` — priority 1 (from `selected-alternatives` default)
- `TestCaseInstanceRepository` — priority 1 (from `@Priority(1)`)

Both are `@ApplicationScoped`, so CDI creates **two separate bean instances**. Each has its own `ConcurrentHashMap<UUID, CaseInstance> store`. If CDI resolves different injection points to different beans (or resolves non-deterministically), saves go to one store and lookups to another.

### Fix
Do not add parent classes to `selected-alternatives` when the engine-testing module already provides `@Priority(1)` subclasses:

```properties
# WRONG — creates priority tie with TestCaseInstanceRepository
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=...,io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository

# RIGHT — let engine-testing's @Priority(1) beans handle it
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=...
# (omit InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository, InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository, InMemoryEventLogRepository)
```

The engine-testing module exists specifically to provide auto-activating alternatives for test contexts. Adding the parent classes to `selected-alternatives` defeats the purpose.

### Why this is non-obvious
Adding a class to `selected-alternatives` feels like an explicit, high-confidence activation — surely it would "win" over a `@Priority(1)` subclass. But the default priority is also 1, creating an exact tie. The Quarkus docs mention the default priority but don't warn about ties with existing `@Priority(1)` alternatives on the classpath. The symptom (state not found) appears as a threading or serialization bug, not a CDI resolution issue.

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives

## Symptom

Test assertions calling `workItemStore.scanAll()` return empty even though the handler log confirms `WorkItemService.create()` ran successfully. `planItem.getStatus()` is `RUNNING` (proving the handler completed), but the store shows nothing.

## Context

Two `WorkItemStore` beans on the test classpath:
- `JpaWorkItemStore @ApplicationScoped` — production bean from `casehub-work`
- `InMemoryWorkItemStore @Alternative @Priority(1)` — test replacement from `casehub-work-testing`

CDI 4.x spec says `@Priority` on an alternative activates it globally and it should win over non-alternatives. In Quarkus ARC 3.32.2 this does not hold when the alternative comes from an external dependency JAR.

## What was tried

- Adding `InMemoryWorkItemStore` to `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` alone → "Ambiguous dependencies" deployment failure (both beans active simultaneously)
- `quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.work.runtime.repository.jpa.JpaWorkItemStore` alone → no effect; `JpaWorkItemStore` still wins
- `@Inject InMemoryWorkItemStore workItemStore` (concrete type) in the test → bypasses CDI alternative selection; test clears the right store but `WorkItemService` still writes to JPA (H2), so `scanAll()` returns empty

## Root Cause

`@Alternative @Priority(1)` from a dependency JAR is not treated as overriding a non-alternative `@ApplicationScoped` bean by Quarkus ARC. CDI spec §2.6.2 requires `@Priority` alternatives to take precedence, but ARC's resolution appears to treat both as equally active candidates when neither is the local application class. The non-alternative wins or ambiguity is raised.

## Fix

Combine both in `src/test/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
# Remove the JPA impl from CDI discovery entirely
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.work.runtime.repository.jpa.JpaWorkItemStore

# Explicitly select the in-memory replacement
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.work.testing.InMemoryWorkItemStore,\
  ...other alternatives...
```

With `JpaWorkItemStore` excluded, `InMemoryWorkItemStore` is the only active `WorkItemStore` bean. Explicit `selected-alternatives` listing then activates it. Both `WorkItemService` (via `@Inject WorkItemStore`) and the test (via `@Inject InMemoryWorkItemStore`) now reference the same singleton, and `scanAll()` finds what `create()` stored.

## Why this is non-obvious

CDI 4.x spec (and Quarkus docs on alternatives) state that `@Alternative @Priority(n)` globally activates and prioritises the alternative. A developer familiar with CDI 4.x would expect `@Priority(1)` to simply work. The failure is silent — no warning is emitted, `JpaWorkItemStore` just wins quietly. The symptom (empty `scanAll()`) points toward a different root cause (session caching, test isolation) and sends debugging in the wrong direction.

**See also:** [GE-20260429-a79d0e](GE-20260429-a79d0e.md) — @Alternative @Priority auto-activates within same module | [GE-20260521-4de4f1](GE-20260521-4de4f1.md) — TestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely

**See also:** [GE-20260601-cee623](GE-20260601-cee623.md) — QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely

**See also:** [GE-20260601-848232](GE-20260601-848232.md) — exclude-types silently does nothing for beans from Jandex-indexed JARs

**See also:** [GE-20260604-2f0889](GE-20260604-2f0889.md) — @Alternative @Priority(N) stubs appear in @All List<T> alongside original bean

**See also:** [GE-20260616-d70e7e](GE-20260616-d70e7e.md) — selected-alternatives silently accepts non-@Alternative beans

entries.title: quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives in application.properties activates @Alternative beans in @QuarkusTest — beans.xml is ignored
entries.body:
This means you must write:
```properties
# CORRECT — no profile prefix; the property applies globally at augmentation
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemorySubCaseGroupRepository
```

Not:
```properties
# WRONG — %test.

is silently ignored for build-time properties; the alternative is not activated
%test.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemorySubCaseGroupRepository
```

The practical consequence: if you add the prefix, your `@Alternative` bean is never selected, and the CDI context boots with the default (or fails with `UnsatisfiedResolutionException`), with no indication of why. All surrounding datasource config in the same file uses `%test.` — the omission looks like a mistake but is required.

**See also:** GE-20260423-a01832 — @Alternative beans in third-party test jars need quarkus.index-dependency | GE-20260422-ebb91d — AmbiguousResolutionException from multiple unqualified @ApplicationScoped beans

**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md) — @Alternative beans in test-helper jar invisible — no Jandex index | [GE-20260427-62d3ab](GE-20260427-62d3ab.md) — @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans as test spies without Mockito

## Update — selected-alternatives DOES NOT WORK for library beans in a Quarkus extension's own @QuarkusTest (2026-06-09)

When the project under test is itself a **Quarkus extension** (has `quarkus-extension.yaml`, deployment module, etc.) rather than a Quarkus application, `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` silently fails to activate `@Alternative` beans from library JARs in `@QuarkusTest` augmentation — even when:
- The library JAR has an embedded Jandex index (`META-INF/jandex.idx`)
- `quarkus.index-dependency` is explicitly configured for that artifact

The symptom is `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` for the `@Alternative` bean's interface at CDI startup. The extension's augmentation does NOT pick up the selected-alternatives property, despite the property appearing in both the main and test `application.properties`.

**Root cause (confirmed via trial):** Extension-mode `@QuarkusTest` augmentation may resolve `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` differently from application-mode augmentation. The exact mechanism is unknown, but the property is simply not effective in this context.

**Workaround — non-`@Alternative` subclass pattern:**

Since CDI inheritance does NOT propagate `@Alternative`, a thin `@ApplicationScoped` subclass of the library's `@Alternative` class is a non-alternative DEFAULT bean:

```java
// In the extension's own runtime module:
@ApplicationScoped
class MyLedgerEntryRepository extends JpaLedgerEntryRepository {
    // No new methods — all behaviour inherited from library's @Alternative class.
    // @ApplicationScoped WITHOUT @Alternative → becomes the DEFAULT CDI bean.
    // CDI injects all @Inject fields from the parent class on this instance.
}
```

This works because:
- The subclass is `@ApplicationScoped` but NOT `@Alternative` → it satisfies injection points as the DEFAULT bean
- CDI discovers it by scanning the extension's own classes (always indexed)
- Parent class's `@Inject` fields are injected normally on the subclass instance
- No `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` config required

**Limitation:** The parent class must have a no-arg constructor (or `@Inject` constructor) to allow subclassing and CDI proxying. Package-private classes cannot be subclassed from a different package.

**When to use this pattern vs selected-alternatives:** Use the subclass pattern when the consuming project is an extension and `selected-alternatives` has proven unreliable. Use `selected-alternatives` for application-mode projects where it reliably works. Refs qhorus#255.

entries.title: persistence-memory/ vs testing/ — in-memory impls belong in persistence-memory/ only when they have a production use case
entries.body:
persistence-memory/ vs testing/ — in-memory impls belong in persistence-memory/ only when they have a production use case

### Symptom
Uncertain whether @Alternative @Priority(1) in-memory implementations belong in
`persistence-memory/` or `testing/` module.

### Context
Casehub platform follows: if in-memory has a production use case, it belongs in
`persistence-memory/`; if it is genuinely test-only, it belongs in `testing/`.

### The decision guide
Ask: *"Could someone reasonably deploy with in-memory persistence in production?"*

- **Yes** (e.g. `WorkItemStore` — demos, local eval without a DB) → `persistence-memory/`
- **No** (e.g. `PreferenceProvider` — file-based `config/` module covers the no-DB case) → `testing/`

### Why this matters
A module named `testing/` signals test-only and may bundle test-framework artifacts.
`persistence-memory/` is a legitimate production deployment target (data is volatile;
restart means data loss, acceptable for evaluation). Conflating them forces consumers
to add a `testing/` module to their production classpath to get ephemeral behaviour.

### Additional pattern
If `key.parse()` or similar enables a string-source provider to return typed values,
an in-memory fixture may be unnecessary entirely — the mock provider handles it via
config strings without a separate module.

### Why this is non-obvious
The natural instinct is to put all `@Alternative @Priority(1)` beans in `testing/`.
The production-deployment use case for in-memory storage is easy to overlook.

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index

## Context

Building a `casehub-testing` module to provide `@Alternative @Priority(1)` in-memory repository implementations that downstream `@QuarkusTest` classes can consume by just adding the module as a test dependency — no `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` config needed.

## Symptom

```
jakarta.enterprise.inject.UnsatisfiedResolutionException: Unsatisfied dependency
  for type io.casehub.engine.spi.CaseInstanceRepository and qualifiers [@Default]
```

Downstream `@QuarkusTest` fails to start even though `casehub-testing` is on the classpath and its `TestCaseInstanceRepository` carries `@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped`.

## Root Cause

Quarkus CDI bean discovery requires a Jandex index to scan beans from classpath JARs. If the testing helper JAR does not build a Jandex index (i.e., the `jandex-maven-plugin` or equivalent is not configured), Quarkus cannot see any of its beans — including `@Alternative @Priority(1)` classes. The annotation is present in the bytecode but the JAR is effectively invisible to CDI.

The `casehub-testing` pom.xml had no Jandex configuration, so its beans were never indexed.

## Fix

**Option A (recommended for reusable testing jars):** Add Jandex indexing to the helper module's pom.xml:

```xml
<plugin>
    <groupId>io.smallrye</groupId>
    <artifactId>jandex-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    <executions>
        <execution>
            <id>make-index</id>
            <goals><goal>jandex</goal></goals>
        </execution>
    </executions>
</plugin>
```

**Option B (proven fallback):** Keep in-memory implementations in `casehub-persistence-memory` without `@Priority`, and activate them via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `src/test/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryEventLogRepository
```

Option B is what every other module in the project already uses and is the safe, proven path.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The `@Alternative @Priority(1)` pattern is supposed to be self-activating — you expect it to "just work" when the JAR is on the classpath. The failure message says "unsatisfied dependency" which points to a missing implementation, not to a missing index. Nothing in the error or Quarkus startup log mentions Jandex.

**See also:** [GE-20260423-a01832](GE-20260423-a01832.md) — @Alternative beans in third-party test jars not scanned without explicit index config | [GE-20260421-03fa72](GE-20260421-03fa72.md) — optional module jar missing Jandex index | [GE-20260417-c59817](GE-20260417-c59817.md) — quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives activates @Alternative beans | [GE-20260427-62d3ab](GE-20260427-62d3ab.md) — @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans as test spies | [GE-20260424-59906a](GE-20260424-59906a.md) — CDI does not scan @ApplicationScoped beans in plain JAR module | [GE-0133](GE-0133.md) — CDI silently ignores beans in jars without Jandex index

**See also:** GE-20260422-ebb91d — CDI AmbiguousResolutionException when multiple @ApplicationScoped beans implement the same SPI interface
**See also:** [GE-20260415-884e48 — @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException](GE-20260415-884e48.md)

entries.title: @IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs
entries.body:
@IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs

### Symptom

Adding a dev-profile-only CDI bean (e.g., a `CurrentPrincipal` for dev mode) via `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` causes startup failures — all beans from the non-profiled `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` list disappear.

### Context

Quarkus profile-specific properties (`%dev.`, `%test.`, `%prod.`) completely **replace** their non-profiled counterparts. If the base `application.properties` has:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  com.example.JpaRepo,\
  com.example.JpaPlanItemStore,\
  com.example.JpaSubCaseGroupRepo
```

Adding `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` drops all three JPA alternatives in dev mode — CDI startup fails with unsatisfied dependency errors for every bean that was in the original list.

### Root cause

Profile-specific properties in Quarkus are full replacements, not merges. This is by design (SmallRye Config behaviour), but the failure mode is silent — no warning that the non-profiled value was dropped.

### Fix

Use bean-level annotations instead of `selected-alternatives`:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
@Priority(150)  // beats OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)
@IfBuildProfile("dev")
public class DemoCurrentPrincipal implements CurrentPrincipal {
    @Override public String tenancyId() { return "demo-tenant"; }
    @Override public String actorId()   { return "demo-user"; }
}
```

**Requirements:**
1. The bean must live in a **Jandex-indexed JAR** (e.g., the application's own `runtime/` module). ArC discovers it automatically — no `selected-alternatives` entry needed.
2. `@IfBuildProfile("dev")` excludes the bean entirely from non-dev builds — no ambiguity in production.
3. `@Priority(150)` resolves over competing `@Alternative` beans (e.g., `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)`) via standard ArC priority resolution.

This is the same mechanism by which `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Alternative @Priority(100)` beats `MockCurrentPrincipal @DefaultBean` in production — without being listed in `selected-alternatives`.

### Why this is non-obvious

The natural instinct is `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=MyDevBean` — it looks like the standard profile-override pattern. The replacement behaviour is documented for SmallRye Config but not prominently flagged in the Quarkus CDI guide. The failure (dropped JPA alternatives) manifests as unrelated `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` errors that don't mention `selected-alternatives` at all.

### Why this fix

`@IfBuildProfile + @Priority` solves the problem at the bean level — no configuration property needed. The alternative (duplicating the full `selected-alternatives` list in every profile override) is fragile and drifts as new alternatives are added.

### Alternatives considered

- **Duplicate the full list in `%dev.`** — works but fragile; every new `@Alternative` bean must be added to every profile-specific override. Drifts silently.
- **Use `@UnlessBuildProfile("prod")` instead of `@IfBuildProfile("dev")`** — activates in both dev and test profiles, which may not be desired. Test profile has its own `FixedCurrentPrincipal @Priority(200)`.

results.query_text: ConcurrentHashMap|CopyOnWriteArrayList|thread.safe|lock.free

entries.title: ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet().add() provides atomic idempotent registration without explicit locks
entries.body:
ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet().add() provides atomic idempotent registration without explicit locks

### Context

You need to register a resource (e.g. a backend in a registry, a channel in a gateway, a listener on a topic) exactly once per key, even under concurrent access from multiple threads. You want to avoid explicit locks, `synchronized` blocks, and the awkward semantics of `computeIfAbsent` when the computation has side effects.

### Technique

```java
private final Set<UUID> registeredKeys = ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet();

public void ensureRegistered(UUID key) {
    if (registeredKeys.add(key)) {   // atomic: exactly one thread returns true
        doRegistration(key);          // called exactly once per key
    }
}

public void deregister(UUID key) {
    registeredKeys.remove(key);      // subsequent ensureRegistered() will re-register
}
```

`ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet()` returns a `Set<K>` backed by a ConcurrentHashMap. `Set.add()` returns `true` if the element was actually added (was absent) and `false` if it was already present. Under concurrent access, exactly one thread gets `true` — all others get `false` and skip the side effect. No explicit locking required.

### Why this is non-obvious

Most developers reach for `synchronized(this)` or `computeIfAbsent`. The `newKeySet().add()` pattern:
- Is 2 lines vs 5-10 for a `synchronized` block
- Returns the boolean signal the caller needs directly from `add()`
- Has guaranteed CAS semantics from the ConcurrentHashMap backing
- Clearly expresses intent: "register this key once, skip if already registered"

`computeIfAbsent` also works but its Javadoc warns that the computation must not modify the map — using it to trigger side effects (registration) is semantically awkward and potentially unsafe if the side effect is not pure. The `Set.add()` pattern has no such restriction.

### Constraints

The side effect in `doRegistration()` must be safe to call after `add()` returns but before other threads observe the registration in the set. If `doRegistration()` itself must be atomic with the `add()` (e.g. the registration step reads back the key), this pattern is insufficient — use a dedicated lock or a more explicit state machine.

entries.title: Read-modify-write on thread-safe store is not atomic — concurrent writers lose updates
ConcurrentHashMap.remove(key, value) uses equals() — reflexive equality on mutable list values creates TOCTOU window
ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent blocks all same-bucket keys while mapping function runs — unusable for network calls
entries.body:
ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent blocks all same-bucket keys while mapping function runs — unusable for network calls

## Symptom

Using `computeIfAbsent` to cache external provider contexts (e.g. fetching a public key from Vault Transit on first use) causes unexpected latency spikes. Under concurrent load, a thread loading one actor's context can block threads loading a different actor's context — even though the keys are completely unrelated.

In extreme cases with synchronised re-entry (the mapping function internally calls back into the map for a different key that hashes to the same bucket), the JVM may deadlock.

## Context

Per-actorId context cache in an `AgentSigner` implementation. First `sign()` call per actor triggers `loadContext(actorId)` which makes an HTTP call to Vault. Subsequent calls should be instant (cache hit).

```java
// WRONG — computeIfAbsent holds the bucket lock during the HTTP call
Optional<C> cached = contextCache.computeIfAbsent(actorId, id -> {
    return loadContext(id);  // HTTP call — may take hundreds of ms
});
```

## Root cause

`ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent` acquires an internal per-bucket lock (striped lock on the bin node) for the duration of the mapping function. While one thread is inside a slow mapping function:
- **All other threads needing any key in the same hash bucket are blocked** — not just the same key
- A second thread calling `computeIfAbsent` for a different key that happens to share the same hash bucket will block until the first completes
- If the mapping function itself calls back into the same map (re-entrant), the JDK **throws `IllegalStateException: Recursive update`** in Java 9+

The Javadoc says "the computation should be short and simple" but gives no hint that slow computations block an entire hash bucket.

## Fix

Use `get()` / `putIfAbsent()` instead. Accept duplicate loads on cold start (two threads for the same key both call `loadContext` — idempotent results are safe):

```java
// CORRECT — no bucket lock held during the network call
Optional<C> cached = contextCache.get(actorId);
if (cached == null) {
    Optional<C> loaded = loadContext(actorId);  // HTTP call, no lock held
    Optional<C> racing = contextCache.putIfAbsent(actorId, loaded);
    cached = racing != null ? racing : loaded;
}
```

`putIfAbsent` is atomic but only holds the lock for the map update itself (microseconds), not for the compute function. The trade-off: on cold start, two threads may both call `loadContext` for the same key. The second's result is discarded (the first writer wins). For idempotent loads (same Vault key → same result), this is safe.

## Why this is non-obvious

The name `computeIfAbsent` implies "compute and store if the key is missing" — developers naturally assume the lock scope is narrow (just the map update). The Javadoc recommends "short and simple" but doesn't say "or you block the entire hash bucket." The symptom (unrelated keys blocking each other) is completely surprising. Developers discovering the issue often suspect their HTTP client or Vault server, not the cache implementation.

`computeIfAbsent` is the correct and safe choice when the mapping function is fast and in-memory. The problem is specifically with slow or blocking mapping functions.

### Why this fix

`putIfAbsent` releases the lock before the network call even starts. The duplicate-load race is the accepted trade-off: two threads load the same key concurrently, one result is discarded. For caches where loading is idempotent and deterministic (fetching a public key from a KMS), this is always safe and preferable to blocking the bucket.

entries.title: Lock-outside-transaction pattern for safe concurrent sequence assignment in CDI/JTA
H2 2.x MVStore MERGE INTO is not concurrent-safe for first-inserts
entries.body:
H2 2.x MVStore MERGE INTO is not concurrent-safe for first-inserts

### Context

`MERGE INTO table USING (...) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT` is used as an
atomic upsert — e.g. for sequence allocation tables that need "insert if absent,
update if present."

### Symptom

Under concurrent writes from multiple threads targeting the same new primary key,
one or more transactions throw:

```
Unique index or primary key violation: "PUBLIC.PK_<table> INDEX ..."
SQL statement: MERGE INTO <table> AS t USING ... WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...
```

The failure only occurs on first-insert for a new key. Subsequent writes (where
the row already exists) hit `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE` and succeed. Serial tests
pass; concurrent tests fail.

### Root Cause

H2 2.0 (July 2021) switched the default storage engine from **page-store**
(table-level locking) to **MVStore** (MVCC / row-level). Under MVStore, `MERGE INTO`
evaluates `WHEN NOT MATCHED` non-atomically:

1. Transaction A reads — row absent → plans INSERT
2. Transaction B reads — row absent → plans INSERT
3. Transaction A inserts → succeeds
4. Transaction B inserts → unique constraint violation (row now exists)

The conflict is not suppressed — it propagates as a `ConstraintViolationException`,
poisoning Transaction B's `EntityManager` and requiring a full transaction rollback.

Documentation and Javadoc written for H2 1.x stated "H2 uses table-level locking."
This was true for page-store. It is false for MVStore.

### Fix

Replace the single-statement `MERGE INTO` with two statements:

```java
// INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING — concurrent first-inserts are safe.
// H2 2.2+ in MODE=PostgreSQL supports the no-column-target form only:
// ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (not ON CONFLICT (col1, col2) DO NOTHING).
em.createNativeQuery(
    "INSERT INTO sequence_table (subject_id, tenancy_id, next_seq) " +
    "VALUES (CAST(?1 AS UUID), ?2, 1) " +
    "ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING")
    .setParameter(1, subjectId).setParameter(2, tenancyId).executeUpdate();

em.createNativeQuery(
    "UPDATE sequence_table SET next_seq = next_seq + 1 " +
    "WHERE subject_id = CAST(?1 AS UUID) AND tenancy_id = ?2")
    .setParameter(1, subjectId).setParameter(2, tenancyId).executeUpdate();
```

Both PostgreSQL and H2 2.2+ (in `MODE=PostgreSQL`) accept this SQL. The `UPDATE`
acquires a row lock held for the transaction duration, serialising concurrent writes.

### Why this is non-obvious

H2 2.0 silently changed the default storage engine — existing `MERGE INTO` code
that worked correctly for years under H2 1.x breaks only under concurrent load.
Serial unit tests always pass. The failure surfaces only in integration test suites
that exercise concurrent writes (e.g. Quartz workers, concurrent HTTP handlers).

The Javadoc claiming "H2 uses table-level locking" remained in the codebase long
after H2 2.x shipped, reinforcing the incorrect assumption.

### Why this fix

`MERGE INTO` evaluated in a single statement appears atomic but is not under MVCC.
The two-statement approach makes the sequence explicit: one statement that safely
handles concurrent first-inserts (DO NOTHING on conflict), and one that serialises
via row lock. This is identical to PostgreSQL's `INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING +
UPDATE` pattern and can be unified across both dialects with the same SQL.

### Alternatives considered

- **`LOCK_MODE=1` on H2 URL** — does not restore table-level locking for MVStore tables;
  the flag only affects the discontinued page-store engine.
- **`REQUIRES_NEW` inner transaction for first-insert** — adds a class and transaction
  boundary just for the initial-insert edge case; rejected as unnecessary complexity.
- **`SELECT FOR UPDATE` / pessimistic lock** — cannot lock a row that doesn't exist yet;
  fails silently on first-insert (same race condition).

**See also:** [GE-20260605-b734b3](GE-20260605-b734b3.md) — SQL-standard MERGE INTO USING WHEN MATCHED/NOT MATCHED for portable H2 + PostgreSQL atomic upsert (the technique that addresses this H2 limitation)

**See also:** [GE-20260618-0ed34c](GE-20260618-0ed34c.md) — H2 LOCK_MODE=1 has no effect with MVStore (failed workaround for this problem) | [GE-20260618-73a023](GE-20260618-73a023.md) — H2 JDBC getMetaData().getURL() drops connection properties

entries.title: ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent blocks all same-bucket keys while mapping function runs — unusable for network calls
entries.body:
ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent blocks all same-bucket keys while mapping function runs — unusable for network calls

## Symptom

Using `computeIfAbsent` to cache external provider contexts (e.g. fetching a public key from Vault Transit on first use) causes unexpected latency spikes. Under concurrent load, a thread loading one actor's context can block threads loading a different actor's context — even though the keys are completely unrelated.

In extreme cases with synchronised re-entry (the mapping function internally calls back into the map for a different key that hashes to the same bucket), the JVM may deadlock.

## Context

Per-actorId context cache in an `AgentSigner` implementation. First `sign()` call per actor triggers `loadContext(actorId)` which makes an HTTP call to Vault. Subsequent calls should be instant (cache hit).

```java
// WRONG — computeIfAbsent holds the bucket lock during the HTTP call
Optional<C> cached = contextCache.computeIfAbsent(actorId, id -> {
    return loadContext(id);  // HTTP call — may take hundreds of ms
});
```

## Root cause

`ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent` acquires an internal per-bucket lock (striped lock on the bin node) for the duration of the mapping function. While one thread is inside a slow mapping function:
- **All other threads needing any key in the same hash bucket are blocked** — not just the same key
- A second thread calling `computeIfAbsent` for a different key that happens to share the same hash bucket will block until the first completes
- If the mapping function itself calls back into the same map (re-entrant), the JDK **throws `IllegalStateException: Recursive update`** in Java 9+

The Javadoc says "the computation should be short and simple" but gives no hint that slow computations block an entire hash bucket.

## Fix

Use `get()` / `putIfAbsent()` instead. Accept duplicate loads on cold start (two threads for the same key both call `loadContext` — idempotent results are safe):

```java
// CORRECT — no bucket lock held during the network call
Optional<C> cached = contextCache.get(actorId);
if (cached == null) {
    Optional<C> loaded = loadContext(actorId);  // HTTP call, no lock held
    Optional<C> racing = contextCache.putIfAbsent(actorId, loaded);
    cached = racing != null ? racing : loaded;
}
```

`putIfAbsent` is atomic but only holds the lock for the map update itself (microseconds), not for the compute function. The trade-off: on cold start, two threads may both call `loadContext` for the same key. The second's result is discarded (the first writer wins). For idempotent loads (same Vault key → same result), this is safe.

## Why this is non-obvious

The name `computeIfAbsent` implies "compute and store if the key is missing" — developers naturally assume the lock scope is narrow (just the map update). The Javadoc recommends "short and simple" but doesn't say "or you block the entire hash bucket." The symptom (unrelated keys blocking each other) is completely surprising. Developers discovering the issue often suspect their HTTP client or Vault server, not the cache implementation.

`computeIfAbsent` is the correct and safe choice when the mapping function is fast and in-memory. The problem is specifically with slow or blocking mapping functions.

### Why this fix

`putIfAbsent` releases the lock before the network call even starts. The duplicate-load race is the accepted trade-off: two threads load the same key concurrently, one result is discarded. For caches where loading is idempotent and deterministic (fetching a public key from a KMS), this is always safe and preferable to blocking the bucket.

entries.title: H2 LOCK_MODE=1 has no effect with MVStore — concurrent MERGE races persist
entries.body:
H2 LOCK_MODE=1 has no effect with MVStore — concurrent MERGE races persist

## Symptom

Adding `LOCK_MODE=1` to the H2 JDBC URL to prevent concurrent first-insert races on a
shared table appears to succeed — no error, the application starts — but the concurrent
race condition continues unaffected. Two threads racing to INSERT the same primary key
still produce `JdbcSQLIntegrityConstraintViolationException`.

```
JDBC URL:
jdbc:h2:mem:testdb;MODE=PostgreSQL;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1;LOCK_MODE=1
```

```
Error (still observed with LOCK_MODE=1):
Unique index or primary key violation: "PUBLIC.PK_LEDGER_SUBJECT_SEQUENCE
INDEX ... VALUES ( /* key:1 */ UUID '...')"; SQL statement:
MERGE INTO ledger_subject_sequence AS t USING (...) AS s ...
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (subject_id, tenancy_id, next_seq) VALUES (...)
```

## Context

Triggered by concurrent `MERGE INTO` on a new row that both threads see as absent (MVCC
read-committed isolation). Common in `@QuarkusTest` suites where multiple Quartz workers
or CDI async observers write to the same H2 table simultaneously for the first time.

LOCK_MODE is a documented H2 parameter. The documentation does not clearly state that it
only applies to the discontinued page-store engine and is silently ignored in MVStore mode.

## What was tried

- `LOCK_MODE=1` in JDBC URL — no effect; race persists.
- `LOCK_MODE=2` — same; still silently ignored.
- Neither URL parameter nor `SET LOCK_MODE` SQL triggers an error; the setting is accepted
  but MVStore's MVCC engine does not honour it.

## Root cause

H2 2.0 switched the default storage engine from page-store to MVStore. MVStore uses MVCC
(multi-version concurrency control) with row-level locking, not table-level locking. The
`LOCK_MODE` URL parameter only controlled the page-store engine's locking behaviour.
In H2 2.x, MVStore always uses MVCC regardless of `LOCK_MODE`. The parameter is accepted
without error for backward compatibility but has no observable effect.

## Fix

Replace `MERGE INTO` with `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` + `UPDATE`. H2 2.2+
(released July 2023) added `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` support for `INSERT` statements,
including in `MODE=PostgreSQL`. The `ON CONFLICT` path is race-safe: if two transactions
simultaneously attempt the first insert, exactly one succeeds and the other silently does
nothing; both then proceed to the `UPDATE`, which serialises via the MVStore row lock.

```sql
-- Instead of:
MERGE INTO t USING (...) AS s ON t.key = s.key
  WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.val = t.val + 1
  WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (key, val) VALUES (s.key, 2)

-- Use:
INSERT INTO t (key, val) VALUES (CAST(? AS UUID), 1) ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;
UPDATE t SET val = val + 1 WHERE key = CAST(? AS UUID);
```

This is the same SQL as the PostgreSQL path — both H2 2.2+ and PostgreSQL support it, so
a single implementation works for both databases.

## Why this is non-obvious

`LOCK_MODE=1` is documented as enabling "table-level locking", which is exactly what is
needed to serialise concurrent MERGE statements. The parameter is silently accepted with no
error or warning when it has no effect. There is no runtime indication that the setting is
being ignored. The fix requires knowing that (a) H2 2.0 removed page-store, (b) MVStore is
MVStore is immune to LOCK_MODE, and (c) H2 2.2+ added ON CONFLICT support — none of these
are prominently documented together.

### Why this fix

`ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` + `UPDATE` produces identical SQL for both H2 and PostgreSQL,
eliminating the dialect split that caused the original bug. The `MERGE INTO` approach
assumed H2 would serialize via table locking — an assumption that was silently wrong for
five major releases.

**See also:** [GE-20260617-6b01c4](GE-20260617-6b01c4.md) — H2 2.x MVStore MERGE INTO is not concurrent-safe for first-inserts (the root problem LOCK_MODE=1 was meant to fix) | [GE-20260618-73a023](GE-20260618-73a023.md) — H2 JDBC getMetaData().getURL() drops connection properties | [GE-20260605-b734b3](GE-20260605-b734b3.md) — SQL-standard MERGE INTO for portable H2 + PostgreSQL
